EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally warm and humid air mass spread across Kansas and Missouri on Friday, thanks in part to a large and powerful storm system which developed across the Great Basin.  Temperatures Friday afternoon soared to records levels in the upper 60s.  In fact, daily high temperature records were set in Kansas City, St. Joseph, Concordia, Joplin, Springfield, Vichy-Rolla, and Des Moines.  This spring-like air mass set the stage for a rapid swing in weather conditions across the area, with extensive snow melt in Iowa creating ice jams and river flooding.||By the evening hours of the 26th, the powerful upper level storm system was beginning to bear down on the Central Plains.  A potent cold front was swinging through Nebraska and Iowa, with the development of thunderstorm activity across much of eastern Kansas and west central Missouri by 9 pm.  This thunderstorm activity, while strong to severe, caused litte in the way of damage, however did produce flash flooding in areas from Warrensburg, MO to Moberly, MO.||After midnight however, a large line of thunderstorms began to develop further to the west along the aforementioned cold front stretching from central Kansas into northwestern Missouri.  Additional stronger thunderstorms developed in areas near Emporia, KS and further southward along Interstate 35 by 3 am.  These thunderstorms would go on to produce widespread wind damage as they lifted northward into the Kansas City Metro and areas to the south and east.  Composite radar imagery below indicates the regional progression of this line of storms and their track across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were broken out by high winds, at a residence located at 159TH and Outlook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and progressive upper level winter storm moved into the Texas panhandle during the overnight hours Wednesday night, January 30th.  An area of snow developed in the Oklahoma panhandle very early Thursday, January 31st, 2008 and moved north into south central Kansas Thursday morning.  When the snow fell, it was heavy at times accompanied by north, gusty winds of 20 to 30 mph, which caused considerable blowing and drifting.  By 11am CST on the 31st, 4 to 5 inches of snow had fallen just east of Medicine Lodge in Barber County, with 2 to 3 inches just west and north of there in Comanche and Pratt counties.  Pratt, Comanche and Barber counties all reported poor driving conditions due to blowing and drifting of snow, with Barber County also reporting schools had closed due to poor road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong and progressive upper level winter storm moved into the Texas panhandle during the overnight hours Wednesday night, January 30th.  An area of snow developed in the Oklahoma panhandle very early Thursday, January 31st, 2008 and moved north into south central Kansas Thursday morning.  When the snow fell, it was heavy at times accompanied by north, gusty winds of 20 to 30 mph, which caused considerable blowing and drifting.  By 11am CST on the 31st, 4 to 5 inches of snow had fallen just east of Medicine Lodge in Barber County, with 2 to 3 inches just west and north of there in Comanche and Pratt counties.  Pratt, Comanche and Barber counties all reported poor driving conditions due to blowing and drifting of snow, with Barber County also reporting schools had closed due to poor road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm moved across the northern plains on Monday, January 28th, 2008, tightening up the surface pressure gradient across southwestern Kansas.  A small part of DDC���s CWA experienced high winds during the late afternoon and early evening of the 28th.  A 40 mph sustained wind was reported from Hugoton in Stevens County at 1833 CST, with another from the same location at 2033 CST of 45 mph gusting to 55 mph.  At Liberal in Seward County, one gust of 60 mph occurred at 1955 CST, with a separate wind report from there of 40 mph gusting to 51 mph at 2115 CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sustained 40 mph wind was reported in Hugoton at 1630 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system at the surface formed over central Kansas during the early morning hours of Thursday, January 10th.  This brought strong northwest winds of 30 to 35 mph to much of central Kansas for a 6 to 10 hour period.  A small band of snow developed in the Hays area (Ellis Co) about midnight Wednesday night.  Since this area had just received a light covering of snow the night before, this new snow had little surface to stick to and just blew across the surface.  Strong northwest winds resulted in blowing snow and near white-out conditions.  Several accidents were reported in and near Hays with cars sliding off the road, and multiple power outages were reported in both Hays and Victoria from downed power lines.   These low visibilities did not last too long (3-4 hours), but did lead to accidents and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system at the surface formed over central Kansas during the early morning hours of Thursday, January 10th.  This brought strong northwest winds of 30 to 35 mph to much of central Kansas for a 6 to 10 hour period.  A small band of snow developed in the Hays area (Ellis Co) about midnight Wednesday night.  Since this area had just received a light covering of snow the night before, this new snow had little surface to stick to and just blew across the surface.  Strong northwest winds resulted in blowing snow and near white-out conditions.  Several accidents were reported in and near Hays with cars sliding off the road, and multiple power outages were reported in both Hays and Victoria from downed power lines.   These low visibilities did not last too long (3-4 hours), but did lead to accidents and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southeast through western Kansas overnight Tuesday night, bringing cold air into the area Wednesday, January 16th, 2008.  A potent upper level storm then crossed from the central Rockies into the central plains Wednesday.  This brought scattered snow showers, some with brief heavy snow, and north winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusty, which caused some areas of blowing snow.  Snowfall of over 2 inches fell in two separate areas of the CWA, one near Hays and the other near Johnson City.  The highest reported snowfall was in Victoria, just east of Hays in Ellis County.  Three inch snows were reported from both La Crosse in Rush County and in Johnson City in Stanton County.  Two inches of snow fell in Sublette in Haskell County and northward into western Finney County and southeastern Kearny County.  Another area of 2 inch snow fell from near Dighton in Lane County east to northern Ellis County and southern Rush County.  Poor visibilities resulted in the blowing snow, with less than 1/8th of a mile visibility reported in Haskell County and less than 1 mile visibility reported in Stanton and Ellis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southeast through western Kansas overnight Tuesday night, bringing cold air into the area Wednesday, January 16th, 2008.  A potent upper level storm then crossed from the central Rockies into the central plains Wednesday.  This brought scattered snow showers, some with brief heavy snow, and north winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusty, which caused some areas of blowing snow.  Snowfall of over 2 inches fell in two separate areas of the CWA, one near Hays and the other near Johnson City.  The highest reported snowfall was in Victoria, just east of Hays in Ellis County.  Three inch snows were reported from both La Crosse in Rush County and in Johnson City in Stanton County.  Two inches of snow fell in Sublette in Haskell County and northward into western Finney County and southeastern Kearny County.  Another area of 2 inch snow fell from near Dighton in Lane County east to northern Ellis County and southern Rush County.  Poor visibilities resulted in the blowing snow, with less than 1/8th of a mile visibility reported in Haskell County and less than 1 mile visibility reported in Stanton and Ellis counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved through the Central Plains spreading severe thunderstorms across South Central Kansas and snow to Central Kansas. Several severe thunderstorms moved across Sedgwick County with hail up to the size of quarters reported during the morning hours of February 5th. The storm produced upwards of 6 inches in that swath with lesser amounts across South Central and Southeast Kansas.

A potent winter storm moved into western Kansas during the early morning of Tuesday, February 5th and then marched eastward through central Kansas during the day Tuesday.  As the upper low tracked west to east through Kansas, a surface low pressure system moved slowly east along the Kansas-Oklahoma border.  This combination resulted in moderate to heavy snow in parts of western and central Kansas.  Before the snow ended, from 6 to 8 inches fell in a 30 to 40 mile-wide swath from Hays to Kalvesta (in Finney County).  An area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell south of this heavier band, and was basically north of a line from Johnson to Jetmore to east of La Crosse in Rush County.  One to two inches of snow fell to the south of this mentioned area, with a few patches of 3-plus inches of snow.  This 1 to 2-inch band was north of a line from Elkhart to Dodge City to Greensburg to south of St. John.  Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported, as north winds blew at 20 to 30 mph during the moderate to heavy snow.  In Gray County just west of Cimarron during the mid-morning of Tuesday, freezing drizzle and fog formed a nearly solid sheet of ice along US Highway 50 along a curve and a hill.  Blowing snow worsened the visibility in that area, and numerous accidents occurred.  Several cars slid off the road, and 2 power poles were downed.  However, no injuries were reported.

One to two inches of snow fell to the south of this mentioned area, with a few patches of 3-plus inches of snow.  This 1 to 2-inch band was north of a line from Elkhart to Dodge City to Greensburg to south of St. John.  Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported, as north winds blew at 20 to 30 mph during the moderate to heavy snow.  In Gray County just west of Cimarron during the mid-morning of Tuesday, freezing drizzle and fog formed a nearly solid sheet of ice along US Highway 50 along a curve and a hill.  Blowing snow worsened the visibility in that area, and numerous accidents occurred.  Several cars slid off the road, and 2 power poles were downed.  However, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and progressive upper level winter storm moved into the Texas panhandle during the overnight hours Wednesday night, January 30th.  An area of snow developed in the Oklahoma panhandle very early Thursday, January 31st, 2008 and moved north into south central Kansas Thursday morning.  When the snow fell, it was heavy at times accompanied by north, gusty winds of 20 to 30 mph, which caused considerable blowing and drifting.  By 11am CST on the 31st, 4 to 5 inches of snow had fallen just east of Medicine Lodge in Barber County, with 2 to 3 inches just west and north of there in Comanche and Pratt counties.  Pratt, Comanche and Barber counties all reported poor driving conditions due to blowing and drifting of snow, with Barber County also reporting schools had closed due to poor road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southeast through western Kansas overnight Tuesday night, bringing cold air into the area Wednesday, January 16th, 2008.  A potent upper level storm then crossed from the central Rockies into the central plains Wednesday.  This brought scattered snow showers, some with brief heavy snow, and north winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusty, which caused some areas of blowing snow.  Snowfall of over 2 inches fell in two separate areas of the CWA, one near Hays and the other near Johnson City.  The highest reported snowfall was in Victoria, just east of Hays in Ellis County.  Three inch snows were reported from both La Crosse in Rush County and in Johnson City in Stanton County.  Two inches of snow fell in Sublette in Haskell County and northward into western Finney County and southeastern Kearny County.  Another area of 2 inch snow fell from near Dighton in Lane County east to northern Ellis County and southern Rush County.  Poor visibilities resulted in the blowing snow, with less than 1/8th of a mile visibility reported in Haskell County and less than 1 mile visibility reported in Stanton and Ellis counties.

A potent winter storm moved into western Kansas during the early morning of Tuesday, February 5th and then marched eastward through central Kansas during the day Tuesday.  As the upper low tracked west to east through Kansas, a surface low pressure system moved slowly east along the Kansas-Oklahoma border.  This combination resulted in moderate to heavy snow in parts of western and central Kansas.  Before the snow ended, from 6 to 8 inches fell in a 30 to 40 mile-wide swath from Hays to Kalvesta (in Finney County).  An area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell south of this heavier band, and was basically north of a line from Johnson to Jetmore to east of La Crosse in Rush County.  One to two inches of snow fell to the south of this mentioned area, with a few patches of 3-plus inches of snow.  This 1 to 2-inch band was north of a line from Elkhart to Dodge City to Greensburg to south of St. John.  Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported, as north winds blew at 20 to 30 mph during the moderate to heavy snow.  In Gray County just west of Cimarron during the mid-morning of Tuesday, freezing drizzle and fog formed a nearly solid sheet of ice along US Highway 50 along a curve and a hill.  Blowing snow worsened the visibility in that area, and numerous accidents occurred.  Several cars slid off the road, and 2 power poles were downed.  However, no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Wakeeney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over NW Kansas during the early evening hours. One long-lived supercell moved slowly east across Sheridan and Graham counties, producing baseball-sized hail and winds of 70-80 mph. Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.

Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.

Another round of severe weather swept across eastern Kansas on July 2, 2008, bringing large hail, damaging winds, and flooding. A long duration severe weather event, began across the Kansas City Metro area, as a cluster of HP Supercells, produced up to baseball sized hail, as well as small bowing segments, producing damaging straight line winds, across many areas of the metro. In addition, torrential rainfall of upward of 6.50 inches fell across portions of the city, resulting in major flash flooding across Leavenworth, and Wyandotte counties.

Heavy rainfall occurring overnight resulted in damage to county roads west of St Francis. One county bridge and several culverts were washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding near Roads P and 6 west of St Francis.  One bridge and several culverts were destroyed.

A winter storm blanketed north central Kansas with a solid 6+ inches of fresh snow.  The snow developed around sunrise and continued throughout the entire day.  Covert reported 8 inches of accumulation, while 7 inches fell at Smith Center, Phillipsburg and near Plainville.  Though winds weren���t overly strong, some blowing and drifting occurred. Many schools across the region were closed at least one day due to the winter weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Cooperative Observer in Smith Center measured 6.8 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved into western Kansas during the early morning of Tuesday, February 5th and then marched eastward through central Kansas during the day Tuesday.  As the upper low tracked west to east through Kansas, a surface low pressure system moved slowly east along the Kansas-Oklahoma border.  This combination resulted in moderate to heavy snow in parts of western and central Kansas.  Before the snow ended, from 6 to 8 inches fell in a 30 to 40 mile-wide swath from Hays to Kalvesta (in Finney County).  An area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell south of this heavier band, and was basically north of a line from Johnson to Jetmore to east of La Crosse in Rush County.  One to two inches of snow fell to the south of this mentioned area, with a few patches of 3-plus inches of snow.  This 1 to 2-inch band was north of a line from Elkhart to Dodge City to Greensburg to south of St. John.  Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported, as north winds blew at 20 to 30 mph during the moderate to heavy snow.  In Gray County just west of Cimarron during the mid-morning of Tuesday, freezing drizzle and fog formed a nearly solid sheet of ice along US Highway 50 along a curve and a hill.  Blowing snow worsened the visibility in that area, and numerous accidents occurred.  Several cars slid off the road, and 2 power poles were downed.  However, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system ejected from the Rockies through the Plains and then into Missouri Saturday, February 23rd.  As the system ejected northeast, precipitation began falling as sleet over a small portion of Dodge City���s CWA Saturday afternoon.  The sleet then changed to snow by late afternoon and even fell as heavy snow for a brief time Saturday evening.  The snow changed to light snow during the night Saturday night, and ended Sunday morning, February 24th.  The Hays and La Crosse areas reported 4 to 5 inches of snow, while from northern Stafford County through northeastern Pawnee County into eastern and northern Ellis County, 2 to 3 inches of snow was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved into western Kansas during the early morning of Tuesday, February 5th and then marched eastward through central Kansas during the day Tuesday.  As the upper low tracked west to east through Kansas, a surface low pressure system moved slowly east along the Kansas-Oklahoma border.  This combination resulted in moderate to heavy snow in parts of western and central Kansas.  Before the snow ended, from 6 to 8 inches fell in a 30 to 40 mile-wide swath from Hays to Kalvesta (in Finney County).  An area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell south of this heavier band, and was basically north of a line from Johnson to Jetmore to east of La Crosse in Rush County.  One to two inches of snow fell to the south of this mentioned area, with a few patches of 3-plus inches of snow.  This 1 to 2-inch band was north of a line from Elkhart to Dodge City to Greensburg to south of St. John.  Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported, as north winds blew at 20 to 30 mph during the moderate to heavy snow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall developed over southern Missouri during the evening of 17 March. A line of training convection assumed a position roughly along a line from Anderson to Ozark to Licking. This convection expanded with time, eventually covering all of Crawford and Cherokee counties. Moderate to heavy rain continued into the overnight period and did not stop until the morning of 19 March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This flooding is a continuation of the flash flooding.  Flooding continued over poor drainage areas for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low level jet propagated to the surface, during the morning hours of March 31, 2008. Light rain evaporating as it fell, combined with the low level jet, to produce wind gusts up to 60 mph, across portions of Johnson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the Johnson County Executive Airport, and a wind gust was measured at 58 mph, at the New Century Airport in Olathe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains associated with a frontal boundary and upper level storm system, caused minor flooding on Stranger Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stranger Creek at Easton crested at 17.99 feet, or 0.99 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and an associated upper level storm system, caused heavy rain and minor river flooding, on the Marais Des Cygnes River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marais Des Cygnes River at La Cygne crested at 26.11 feet, or 1.11 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and an associated upper level storm system, caused heavy rain and minor river flooding, on the Marais Des Cygnes River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marais Des Cygnes River at Osawatomie crested at 28.06 feet, or 0.06 of a foot above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary in eastern Colorado and northwest Kansas during the afternoon and evening, resulting in an isolated tornado, heavy rainfall and a few severe wind reports.

Slow-moving thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary in eastern Colorado and northwest Kansas during the afternoon and evening, resulting in an isolated tornado, heavy rainfall and a few severe wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was visible from the Goodland NWS office and was observed for approximately 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind damage across Norton county during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches 3-4 inches in diameter were broken. A grain bin was damaged and a wall from an old wooden barn was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Dolly drifted across eastern Colorado and western Kansas, producing locally heavy rainfall and a few isolated severe wind and hail reports along the Colorado-Kansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen large trees were downed as a result of strong thunderstorm winds during the late afternoon/early evening hours along Hackberry Creek.

Hail up to the size of quarters and wind gusts to 60 mph occurred with the storms. The slow moving storms also produced flooding in Chase County near the town of Cedar Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds picked up suddenly from the south.

A surface boundary draped across the region provided a focus for thunderstorm development as an upper level disturbance crossed the Central Plains.  These thunderstorms developed during the late afternoong and early evening hours, and conditions were favorable for thunderstorms to become severe.  Hail up to the size of a hen egg was reported across portions of north central Kansas, along with wind gusts estimated up to 80 mph.  A low level jet nosing into the area helped to sustain convection through midnight, after which thunderstorms moved south out of the area.

The low pressure system that brought severe weather to Kansas on May 1st deepened significantly by May 2nd. This strong surface low lead to stiff northwest winds across Central and South Central Kansas. Wind gusts were generally in the 50 to 55 mph range however, the Hutchinson ASOS reported a wind gusts of 60 mph and there were reports of minor tree damage in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gusts occurred at the Hutchinson ASOS. There was also minor tree damage reported in the city.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed a tree onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms developed over Northwest Kansas on the evening and overnight of June 18th, 2008.  The complex of storms lead to a quasi linear convective system moving south across portions of Central and South Central Kansas,  producing damaging winds along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wind gust was measured at the KSNW weather lab at Argonia Elementary School.

Yet another round of severe weather occurred but this time in only an isolated area, mainly near Sublette in Haskell county and in northern Stevens county.  Winds aloft were weak and allow the small cluster to dump copious amounts of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain produced a tremendous amount of runoff that caused the sewer system in Sublette to overrun it's capacity.  The residents were asked not to use facilities for about 48 hours after the event.

An anonymously strong upper level system allowed everything to come together at the surface to produced what is perhaps the biggest tornado outbreak to ever occur in the Dodge City CWA!  Fifty-five tornadoes were documented during that afternoon and evening!  Some of the tornadoes were very large and damaging.  The character of the supercell thunderstorms that day had similarities to the storms that produced the Greensburg tornado a little over a year after.  In fact there was one tornado that was just as large and perhaps could have been just as damaging that was headed towards the small Kiowa county town but fortunately turned and dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate tree damage was reported in a wind break.

A very moist airmass was in place during the day of August 9th with a lower level boundary draped from Southeast Kansas through Northern Kansas. As low-level winds increased overnight and interacted with this boundary, heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms developed. Heavy rains from several nights before acted as primer event and with wides spread 2 to 4 inch rain amounts from 9th through the 10th flash flooding occurred in several locations in Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over Highway 99 near mile marker 49 about 5 miles south of Climax.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very hot and muggy day in which temperatures soared into the upper 80s to middle 90s, and dew points rose into the 70s across much of Missouri and Kansas, a weak cold front interacted with an upper level disturbance, moving across the northern plains and pushed south into central and southern Missouri. The combination of the frontal boundary and the upper level energy, provided enough lift to allow several strong to severe thunderstorms to form, during the late afternoon and evening hours of Thursday, August 28th. A couple of large hail events were reported.

Isolated to widely scattered thunderstorms developed across Central and South Central Kansas along a weak area of convergence.  Lots of low level moisture lead to diurnal thunderstorms and some isolated severe weather.

A slow moving cold front and moisture along this frontal boundary lead to some severe thunderstorms across Southeast Kansas.  The slow moving boundary lead to the early morning thunderstorms festering over Southeast Kansas, leading to some heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over Kansas Highway 47 between US highway 169 and US highway 59.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall washed out a culvert between Walters and Winterset Roads along 192nd Street south of Bunker Hill.

An anonymously strong upper level system allowed everything to come together at the surface to produced what is perhaps the biggest tornado outbreak to ever occur in the Dodge City CWA!  Fifty-five tornadoes were documented during that afternoon and evening!  Some of the tornadoes were very large and damaging.  The character of the supercell thunderstorms that day had similarities to the storms that produced the Greensburg tornado a little over a year after.  In fact there was one tornado that was just as large and perhaps could have been just as damaging that was headed towards the small Kiowa county town but fortunately turned and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anonymously strong upper level system allowed everything to come together at the surface to produced what is perhaps the biggest tornado outbreak to ever occur in the Dodge City CWA!  Fifty-five tornadoes were documented during that afternoon and evening!  Some of the tornadoes were very large and damaging. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This extremely large tornado (1.8 miles wide) moved east and then turned northeast towards Kiowa county. Many power poles were taken down by the tornado.  It also caused EF3 damage to trees, two old houses, barns and it carried an oil tank battery 1.8 miles depositing it in trees.   There was at least 20 head of cattle killed by the tornado.  There was minor injuries received by a 20 year old male as he took refuge in his truck that was parked next to a baler inside a barn.  The barn was completely swept away leaving the young man with cuts from broken glass.  The baler that was left standing probably protected him and his truck from going airborne, if not rolling.  Also, there was serious injury to a male in a semi on highway 34 in the vicinity of 37.3327 N, 99.6344 W probably by the RFD.  Two other semis parked at this location rolled over but no injuries were sustained by the drivers.  This extremely large tornado moved into Kiowa county.  The radar signature was eerily similar to the Greensburg tornado that occurred on May 4, 2007.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anonymously strong upper level system allowed everything to come together at the surface to produced what is perhaps the biggest tornado outbreak to ever occur in the Dodge City CWA!  Fifty-five tornadoes were documented during that afternoon and evening!  Some of the tornadoes were very large and damaging.  The character of the supercell thunderstorms that day had similarities to the storms that produced the Greensburg tornado a little over a year after.  In fact there was one tornado that was just as large and perhaps could have been just as damaging that was headed towards the small Kiowa county town but fortunately turned and dissipated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed southwest of the Wakeeney airport and subsequently moved across the south side of Wakeeney before curving north along 13th Avenue and dissipating about 0.25 miles north of the city limit and 0.25 miles west of U.S. 283 (13th Street). The first damage was destruction of a hangar at the airport. The hangar was open to the south, and the wind entered the structure, lifted it, and deposited it in pieces northeast of its original location. The tornado moved through the KOA Campground south of I70, rolling a fifth wheel trailer, destroying a small cabin that was held down only by gravity, blowing limbs up to six inches in diameter from trees, and removing shingles from the roof of the manager���s office. Portions of the flat roof covering on the high school were lifted and peeled off. Small storage buildings were destroyed and trees were uprooted along 13th Street. At the north end of Wakeeney, a large Quonset building that opened to the south was lifted and destroyed, and the roof was removed from a brick barn.  Wind speeds were estimated to be 90-100 mph.

The character of the supercell thunderstorms that day had similarities to the storms that produced the Greensburg tornado a little over a year after.  In fact there was one tornado that was just as large and perhaps could have been just as damaging that was headed towards the small Kiowa county town but fortunately turned and dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was making contact over open range land.  It was witnessed by storm spotters and the Emergency Manager.

Fifty-five tornadoes were documented during that afternoon and evening!  Some of the tornadoes were very large and damaging.  The character of the supercell thunderstorms that day had similarities to the storms that produced the Greensburg tornado a little over a year after.  In fact there was one tornado that was just as large and perhaps could have been just as damaging that was headed towards the small Kiowa county town but fortunately turned and dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado apparently did not strike anything.

An intense jet stream in the middle levels of the atmosphere mixed down to the surface with day time heating, resulting in a day of strong, gusty north winds across the region. All counties experienced wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph with several counties gusting near 60 mph.  No damage was reported.

Moisture plume from the Southwest United States spread northeast towards the plains and interacted with a stationary boundary to produce a deluge of rainfall across Central and South Central Kansas.  Rainfall amounts were generally around 5 inches, however isolated amounts reached up to 11.00 inches on the West side of Wichita, Kansas.  Wichita Mid Continent Airport reported the greatest 24 rainfall total (10.31 inches) in its history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 10.80 inches caused widespread flooding across Valley Center.  Hit the hardest was the Praire Lakes subdivision, where the large amount of rain caused the retention pond around the subdivision to rise rapidly and flood basements and stall cars. The Valley Center school district dismissed students early due to the high water and blocked access in and out of Valley Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain resulted in numerous traffic accidents in the area. The town of Oakley was without power for approximately 90 minutes during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture plume from the Southwest United States spread northeast towards the plains and interacted with a stationary boundary to produce a deluge of rainfall across Central and South Central Kansas.  Rainfall amounts were generally around 5 inches, however isolated amounts reached up to 11.00 inches on the West side of Wichita, Kansas.  Wichita Mid Continent Airport reported the greatest 24 rainfall total (10.31 inches) in its history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to twelve inches of rainfall during the morning commute hours causing widespread street flooding of roadways up to 2 to 3 feet deep on the West side of the City of Wichita. Law enforcement officials reported 150 people were rescued from vehicles or evacuated because of the high water.  114 homes reported some type of surface flooding due to the heavy rainfall and sewers and sump pumps backed up into 141 more homes.  This unprecedented amount of rainfall mainly fell during a five hour period, reminding some of the Halloween Flood of 1998.  The large amount of rainfall caused the Cowskin/Calfskin Creek to once again come out of its banks, flooding homes in the Dells and Hidden Lakes Subdivision.  35 people were evacuated from the Park West Retirement home along the banks of the creek. Calls about stalled vehicles overwhelmed towing businesses across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure area developed over the Central Plains states.  Northwest winds of 45 to 70 mph developed in the strong surface pressure gradient west of the low pressure system which affected eastern Colorado, western Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Several locations in northwest Kansas reported downed power lines and two vehicle rollovers were reported on Interstate 70 in Sherman county Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass was in place during the day of August 9th with a lower level boundary draped from Southeast Kansas through Northern Kansas. As low-level winds increased overnight and interacted with this boundary, heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms developed. Heavy rains from several nights before acted as primer event and with wides spread 2 to 4 inch rain amounts from 9th through the 10th flash flooding occurred in several locations in Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over Kansas Highway 14 about 6 miles north of Ellsworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first snowfall of the season lead to numerous accidents across South Central and Southeast Kansas, as an upper level disturbance moved across the area.  This upper level disturbance lead to 1 to 3 inches of snow across portions of Southern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell across portions of the county leading to numerous traffic accidents.  Over 650 traffic accidents were reported in and an around the Greater Wichita area during the morning commute, with 36 injuries reported. One accident of note was a 5 to 6 car pileup near Park City along Interstate 135.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overunning precipitation over the top of a cold airmass, combined to produce two rounds of wintery precipitation across portions of Central and South Central Kansas late on December 15th into the early portion of December 16th, 2008. Two to five inches of snowfall combined with a thin layer of ice to produce numerous accidents and one death due to hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow produced slick conditions across portions of Southeast Kansas.  One such such accident near Fredonia involved a  vehicles at a railrood crossing. Once the vehicle crossed the crossing it lost control and rolled into the ditch.  A passenger in the vehicle was injured and taken to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first snowfall of the season lead to numerous accidents across South Central and Southeast Kansas, as an upper level disturbance moved across the area.  This upper level disturbance lead to 1 to 3 inches of snow across portions of Southern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow lead to numerous accidents across the county. A 56 year man died near El Dorado, KS., when he lost control of his pickup truck that rolled over.  Another accident, near the Augusta airport, injured two people as the vehicle they were driving left the highway and hit a parked recreational vehicle.  The driver of the vehicle had to be extricated and flown to a hospital in Wichita.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow caused numerous accidents across the county.  Even with all the accidents only one injury was reported because of a car sliding off the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell across portions of the county resulting in numerous accidents. One such accident killed a man, when he lost control of his car and hit a tree at a high rate of speed on a city street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of snowfall caused several accidents across the county.  One such accident, injured a teenager, when his car slid into the path of a semi-tractor trailer along Kansas highway 96 near Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overunning precipitation over the top of a cold airmass, combined to produce two rounds of wintery precipitation across portions of Central and South Central Kansas late on December 15th into the early portion of December 16th, 2008. Two to five inches of snowfall combined with a thin layer of ice to produce numerous accidents and one death due to hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snowfall lead to numerous accidents across portions of Southeast Kansas.  Five such accidents occurred across the county, but the most serious happened when a jeep with four teenagers slid into a fence and rolled over.  Only minor injuries occurred with the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first snowfall of the season lead to numerous accidents across South Central and Southeast Kansas, as an upper level disturbance moved across the area.  This upper level disturbance lead to 1 to 3 inches of snow across portions of Southern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snowfall caused a total of ten accidents across portions of the county.  Two of the accidents were injury accidents when the vehicles they were driving overturned on slick highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. The icy conditions aided numerous vehicle accidents, two of which were fatal across Sumner and Cowley counties, and several of which produced injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions due to light freezing rain, freezing drizzle and light snow aided in an injury accident on K-96 a few miles south of South Hutchinson at 7:45 am on the 24th. A 55-year-old Yoder woman was westbound when her pickup hit a patch of ice, striking another pickup driven by a 32-year-old South Hutchinson man. Both were injured in the accident. Information courtesy of the Hutchinson News.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. The icy conditions aided numerous vehicle accidents, two of which were fatal across Sumner and Cowley counties, and several of which produced injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions due to light freezing rain, freezing drizzle and light snow aided in a 24-year-old Hutchinson woman to lose control of her vehicle somewhere near Wichita on the morning of the 24th. The vehicle hit a fence and rolled once, injuring the woman. In another car accident, a 23-year-old Garden City man was injured on K-96 near 263rd Street around 10 am on the 24th, when another vehicle lost control and spun into his. Information courtesy of the Hutchinson News.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. The icy conditions aided numerous vehicle accidents, two of which were fatal across Sumner and Cowley counties, and several of which produced injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions due to light freezing rain and freezing drizzle aided in a single-vehicle injury accident on I-135 north of Newton around 11:30 pm on the 23rd. The pickup lost control on the ice and rolled a few times in the median. Information courtesy of the Hutchinson News.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms surged east across portions of south-central and southeast Kansas during the predawn hours on the 27th, as a strong storm system and associated cold front approached from the west. Scattered occurrences of dime size hail and 60 mph winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms sparked a house fire in the community of Colony during the predawn hours of the 27th. The residence was completely destroyed. Fortunately, no one was home at the time. Information courtesy of the Iola Register.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air overrunning a retreating cold airmass resulted in widespread freezing drizzle, freezing rain and fog across south-central and southeast Kansas during the predawn and morning hours of December 18th. While only minor ice accumulations occurred, it was enough to cause 3 known injury car accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roads due to freezing drizzle and fog during the early morning hours resulted in an injury car accident one-quarter mile south of Arkansas Road on 1200 Street, about 4 miles south of Humboldt. A Humboldt woman lost control of her vehicle while traveling southbound on 1200 Street. She eventually struck a Humboldt man in his vehicle, and finally collided head-on with a Chanute man in his vehicle. Information courtesy of the Humboldt Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air overrunning a retreating cold airmass resulted in widespread freezing drizzle, freezing rain and fog across south-central and southeast Kansas during the predawn and morning hours of December 18th. While only minor ice accumulations occurred, it was enough to cause 3 known injury car accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roads due to freezing drizzle and foggy conditions during the early morning hours resulted in two injury accidents. The first one occurred just before 9 am when a 33 year old Belle Plaine man lost control of his pickup on a Highway 160 bridge about 12 miles east of Harper. The man was ejected from the cab after the pickup rolled several times. The second accident occurred about 20 minutes later at nearly the same location. A Harper man was driving a semi truck westbound on Highway 160 when he saw the previous accident ahead of him. When he started braking, the semi slid into the ditch. Information courtesy of the Harper Advocate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. The icy conditions aided numerous vehicle accidents, two of which were fatal across Sumner and Cowley counties, and several of which produced injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions due to light freezing rain and freezing drizzle aided in a fatal car accident shortly after 9 am just north of the Belle Plaine service center on the Kansas Turnpike. A 39-year-old Clearwater man and his 9-year-old son were traveling north on I-35 when the pickup rolled into the ditch after they lost control in the icy conditions. Sadly, the father died at the scene, while the boy sustained some injuries. Another accident occurred around 8:45 am when an SUV rolled near GKN Aerospace on Highway 81. About 30 minutes later, a semi-truck fell partially off a bridge on Highway 160, next to the Wellington Wal-Mart. Several other accidents, some with injuries, occurred across Sumner County on the 23rd due to the inclement weather. Information courtesy of the Wichita Eagle, Arkansas City Traveler and Wellington News.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. The icy conditions aided numerous vehicle accidents, two of which were fatal across Sumner and Cowley counties, and several of which produced injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions due to light freezing rain and freezing drizzle aided in a three-vehicle accident on the 23rd shortly after 10 pm on I-135 just south of Crawford Street in Salina, resulting in two injuries. Information courtesy of the Salina Journal. Several other accidents were reported on I-70, but any other possible injuries remain unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overrunning colder air at the surface resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas from the 23rd through the early morning hours on the 24th. The icy conditions aided numerous vehicle accidents, two of which were fatal across Sumner and Cowley counties, and several of which produced injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions due to light freezing rain and freezing drizzle aided in a fatal car accident around 9:30 am on Highway 77, 10 miles north of Winfield. A 37-year-old El Dorado man was injured when he lost control of the south-bound van and hit a tree, engulfing the van in flames. One of the passengers, a 19-year-old El Dorado man, was also injured. Sadly, the other passenger, a 61-year-old El Dorado man, was killed. Additionally, a Kansas Department of Wildlife and Parks officer and a Highway Patrol Trooper sustained injures attempting to pull the 61-year-old El Dorado man from the van. Several other accidents, some with injuries, occurred on the 23rd across Cowley County due to the inclement weather. Information courtesy of the Wichita Eagle and Arkansas City Traveler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered light snow showers lead to icy streets across portions of Southeast Kansas during the early morning hours of December 1st, 2008.  The icy roads lead to a few accidents, with one accident resulting in a mans death as his pickup overturned and caught fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers produced slick roads across portions of the county to the south of Iola. One accident involved a pickup pulling a travel trailer, that lost control on an icy bridge on Highway 169. The pickup lost control, overturned and caught fire. All 3 passengers were ejected from the pickup. One 34 year old man died and 2 others had serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying lee side trough along the front range of the Rockies and a strong surface high pressure area in the Ozark Mountains combined to produce a tight pressure gradient across South Central and Southeast Kansas. This tight gradient produced strong wind gusts to 50 to 60 mph near Coffeyville, Winfield and Chanute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down in Altoona due to high winds.

Low-topped convection in a highly sheared environment brought a narrow path of straight line wind damage from South Central Coffey County beginning around 11:45 pm on January 7th and extending Northeast through West Central and Central Anderson County through approximately 12:40 am on January 8th.  Damage was done to trees and power poles and a couple of outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter had digital anemometer mounted four to five feet above the roof of his house.  Large tree limbs were downed on his property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across the central and southern plains on January 31st. This resulted in a relatively narrow swath of heavy snow across south-central and southeast Kansas. The snow started falling after midnight on the 31st, and ended by mid-afternoon across south-central Kansas, and early evening across southeast Kansas. The snow was particularly heavy during the morning hours, when snowfall rates exceeded one inch per hour for several hours, especially across southeast Kansas. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches for Kansas counties bordering Oklahoma, with 4 to 6 inches one row of counties farther north. Additionally, gusty northerly winds up to 35 mph resulted in reduced visibilities and at times significant drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 7 inches in Rock, and 6 inches in Winfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 5 inches across the county. North winds gusting to 30 mph resulted in reduced visibilities and drifting snow. Two vehicle accidents occurred across the county due to the heavy snow, resulting in 4 injuries (indirect). A 64-year-old woman was critically injured (indirect) when her westbound van collided head-on with an eastbound pick-up on U.S. Highway 54 just west of the Greenwood County line. The driver of the pick-up, a 28-year-old man was also injured (indirect). Another injury accident occurred on Butler Road near 60th north of Andover. A southbound SUV driven by a 42-year-old female collided with a northbound car driven by a 56-year-old male. Both drivers were injured (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms rumbled across north central Kansas during the early morning hours and produced pea to penny size hail.

Increasing moisture in the low layers began to overrun a shallow arctic airmass already in place across portions of South Central and Southeast Kansas during the early morning hours of February 11th, 2008.  This warm air aloft lead to some of the rain turning to sleet with some of the rain freezing on contact.  Some rumbles of thunder were also heard, which increased the accumulation of sleet and freezing rain across Southeast Kansas.  Some pea to dime size hail also accompanied some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced rapid accumulations of freezing rain and sleet across portions of the county.  Two of the hardest hit communities included Sedan and Hale, where a half an inch of ice accumulated on trees and roads.  Sleet accumulations were less than a half an inch.

Some rumbles of thunder were also heard, which increased the accumulation of sleet and freezing rain across Southeast Kansas.  Some pea to dime size hail also accompanied some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced rapid accumulations of freezing and sleet across portions of the county.  Southeast portions of the county received up to a half an inch of ice accumulation on trees and roads. Sleet accumulations were generally less than a half an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southeast through western Kansas overnight Tuesday night, bringing cold air into the area Wednesday, January 16th, 2008.  A potent upper level storm then crossed from the central Rockies into the central plains Wednesday.  This brought scattered snow showers, some with brief heavy snow, and north winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusty, which caused some areas of blowing snow.  Snowfall of over 2 inches fell in two separate areas of the CWA, one near Hays and the other near Johnson City.  The highest reported snowfall was in Victoria, just east of Hays in Ellis County.  Three inch snows were reported from both La Crosse in Rush County and in Johnson City in Stanton County.  Two inches of snow fell in Sublette in Haskell County and northward into western Finney County and southeastern Kearny County.  Another area of 2 inch snow fell from near Dighton in Lane County east to northern Ellis County and southern Rush County.  Poor visibilities resulted in the blowing snow, with less than 1/8th of a mile visibility reported in Haskell County and less than 1 mile visibility reported in Stanton and Ellis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southeast through western Kansas overnight Tuesday night, bringing cold air into the area Wednesday, January 16th, 2008.  A potent upper level storm then crossed from the central Rockies into the central plains Wednesday.  This brought scattered snow showers, some with brief heavy snow, and north winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusty, which caused some areas of blowing snow.  Snowfall of over 2 inches fell in two separate areas of the CWA, one near Hays and the other near Johnson City.  The highest reported snowfall was in Victoria, just east of Hays in Ellis County.  Three inch snows were reported from both La Crosse in Rush County and in Johnson City in Stanton County.  Two inches of snow fell in Sublette in Haskell County and northward into western Finney County and southeastern Kearny County.  Another area of 2 inch snow fell from near Dighton in Lane County east to northern Ellis County and southern Rush County.  Poor visibilities resulted in the blowing snow, with less than 1/8th of a mile visibility reported in Haskell County and less than 1 mile visibility reported in Stanton and Ellis counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm blanketed north central Kansas with a solid 6+ inches of fresh snow.  The snow developed around sunrise and continued throughout the entire day.  Covert reported 8 inches of accumulation, while 7 inches fell at Smith Center, Phillipsburg and near Plainville.  Though winds weren���t overly strong, some blowing and drifting occurred. Many schools across the region were closed at least one day due to the winter weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six inches of new snow was reported in Jewell County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front blasted south across south-central Kansas during the morning hours of January 29th, resulting in very strong and gusty north winds and a brief period of light to moderate snow. The strong winds overturned a cattle tractor-trailer in Cowley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds gusting to 50 mph blew over a cattle tractor trailer on U.S. Highway 160 just east of Oxford. The driver sustained minor injuries (indirect). Several head of cattle perished in the accident, but most of the 33 on board were saved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and moist day set the stage for a severe thunderstorm outbreak across parts of the Central and Southern Plains. The hardest hit areas were the Missouri Ozarks and northeast Oklahoma. However, Southeast Kansas saw several thunderstorms blossom ahead the main storm system. The storms produced up to one inch hail and a knocked down several power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and somewhat moist air over-ran a frontal boundary to produce areas of light freezing drizzle and freezing rain across south-central Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of January 21st. Ice accumulations were light and relatively minor, but enough to create very slick conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very slick conditions due to light freezing drizzle and freezing rain resulted in an injury accident about one mile east of SE 150th Ave. A 49-year-old woman was injured (indirect) when her vehicle slid into the ditch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 7 inches of snow accumulated across southeast portions of the county, with 3 to 4 inches accumulating in the city of Kingman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across the central and southern plains on January 31st. This resulted in a relatively narrow swath of heavy snow across south-central and southeast Kansas. The snow started falling after midnight on the 31st, and ended by mid-afternoon across south-central Kansas, and early evening across southeast Kansas. The snow was particularly heavy during the morning hours, when snowfall rates exceeded one inch per hour for several hours, especially across southeast Kansas. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches for Kansas counties bordering Oklahoma, with 4 to 6 inches one row of counties farther north. Additionally, gusty northerly winds up to 35 mph resulted in reduced visibilities and at times significant drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 inches in Fredonia, but likely accumulated near 6 inches across extreme southwest portions of the county. An 8-year-old boy was injured (indirect) when the car he was riding in lost control in the snow and slammed into a snowbank on northbound U.S. Highway 400 near the Granby Street turnoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture in the low layers began to overrun a shallow arctic airmass already in place across portions of South Central and Southeast Kansas during the early morning hours of February 11th, 2008.  This warm air aloft lead to some of the rain turning to sleet with some of the rain freezing on contact.  Some rumbles of thunder were also heard, which increased the accumulation of sleet and freezing rain across Southeast Kansas.  Some pea to dime size hail also accompanied some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced rapid accmulations of ice across the county with the town of Oswego picking up a half an inch on trees and powerlines.  Sleet also accompanied the thunderstorms with accumalations around a half an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial shortwave trough spurred a cold front to dive through the region late the evening of the 8th through the morning of the 9th.  Warm advection rain showers changed over to snow as the system passed through, with between one and three inches common over portions of east central Kansas. Dozens of accidents occurred along the roadways of several counties.  One vehicle crash fatality was attributed to the weather, as were numerous reported injuries that occurred as a result of wrecks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than two dozen accidents were reported across Coffey County, four of which resulted in minor injuries to individuals in vehicles that had rolled due to the click road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the region during the afternoon and evening hours producing 10 tornadoes, including an F4 tornado just SW of Quinter. Extremely heavy rainfall also resulted in flooding in many areas. Many of these thunderstorms occurred in the same areas as the storms the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall occurred in association with several rounds of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours.  County roads were reported impassible in many areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice storm brought significant accumulations of freezing rain and sleet to extreme southeast Kansas. Accumulations of one quarter to three quarters of an inch were common. The primary precipitation type was sleet and was accompanied by thunder and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of sleet fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved through the Central Plains spreading severe thunderstorms across South Central Kansas and snow to Central Kansas. Several severe thunderstorms moved across Sedgwick County with hail up to the size of quarters reported during the morning hours of February 5th. As the storm tapped into colder air on heavy snow fell from Russell through Chase counties. The storm produced upwards of 6 inches in that swath with lesser amounts across South Central and Southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Russell County law enforcement officials estimated that 6 inches of snow had fallen across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of light freezing rain, sleet and light snow affected central, south-central and southeast Kansas on February 23rd, as an upper level disturbance moved over the area. Snow accumulated from generally central into east-central Kansas, with accumulations ranging from 1 to 5 inches, greatest over central Kansas. Ice and sleet accumulations were very light, but in concert with the snow was enough to cause some travel headaches. Numerous accidents occurred, some of which caused injuries (indirect) across central Kansas, and one of which was fatal (indirect) in Allen county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain during the afternoon hours of February 23rd eventually changed over to all snow during the evening hours, and accumulated up to 2 inches across Allen County before ending around 11 p.m. Associated slick road conditions led to a fatal car accident (indirect) on Highway 54 near Iola around 8:30 p.m. The 32-year-old Iola woman was killed when she lost control of her vehicle. Information courtesy The Wichita Eagle.

The area received about 2 inches of snow, along with light freezing rain and sleet accumulations. Information courtesy The Salina Journal.

Snow accumulated about 4 inches, while ice and sleet accumulations remained light. Information courtesy The Great Bend Tribune.

The pickup driver, a 47-year-old Manhattan woman, slid across the center line and collided with a semi-trailer, injuring herself and two passengers: an 18-year-old Manhattan woman, and an 18-year-old Hays man. Four to five inches of snow accumulated across the area, with ice and sleet accumulations remaining light. Information courtesy The Great Bend Tribune.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong 500mb storm system dug into the four corners region the morning of February 5th, while a surface low established itself over northwest Texas.  A cold front extended from the surface low northeastward. Southerly midlevel winds overrode this boundary, and precipitation developed through the day of the 5th. Cold advection north of the front also brought snow to portions of north central Kansas during the morning. The precipitation type along and south of the boundary in the morning was rain, with a few embedded thunderstorms. There was a brief lull in the precipitation when the low level jet slackened during the late morning. The upper and surface lows lifted to the northeast during the early afternoon, and the cold front established itself along a southwest to northeast line nearly bisecting the local area. Temperatures north of the boundary hovered right around to a few degrees below freezing, while temperatures south of the front were above the freezing mark near the surface and aloft. The front gradually slipped eastward through the day, allowing temperatures to gradually drop below freezing.  The precipitation type switched over to all snow by mid afternoon.  A band of heavy snow established itself from near Brown County to near Morris County for the remainder of the day, and through the night within the axis of moisture advection around the north and west side of the low.  Eight to tweleve inches of snow was reported to have fallen in association with this band, while 3 to four inch amounts were common to the north and south.  Gusty fifteen to twenty five mile per hour winds exacerbated the reduced visibilities within the heavy snow region.  Accidents were reported across the warning area, but none were serious, with mainly slid-offs reported.  Schools across northeast Kansas were closed as a result of the heavy snowfall, including the University of Kansas. One of the main concerns during this busy winter for several cities was the availabilty of road salt.  Many cities were nearly out of salt by the time this snow storm hit, forcing officials to use a mix of salt and sand on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was reported to have fallen in Marysville.  Six to eight inches of snowfall was common across the rest of the county.

Many cities were nearly out of salt by the time this snow storm hit, forcing officials to use a mix of salt and sand on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow was reported to have accumulated across the county.  Gusty winds in combination with the snow lead to reduced visibilities and slick roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong 500mb storm system dug into the four corners region the morning of February 5th, while a surface low established itself over northwest Texas.  A cold front extended from the surface low northeastward. Southerly midlevel winds overrode this boundary, and precipitation developed through the day of the 5th. Cold advection north of the front also brought snow to portions of north central Kansas during the morning. The precipitation type along and south of the boundary in the morning was rain, with a few embedded thunderstorms. There was a brief lull in the precipitation when the low level jet slackened during the late morning. The upper and surface lows lifted to the northeast during the early afternoon, and the cold front established itself along a southwest to northeast line nearly bisecting the local area. Temperatures north of the boundary hovered right around to a few degrees below freezing, while temperatures south of the front were above the freezing mark near the surface and aloft. The front gradually slipped eastward through the day, allowing temperatures to gradually drop below freezing.  The precipitation type switched over to all snow by mid afternoon.  A band of heavy snow established itself from near Brown County to near Morris County for the remainder of the day, and through the night within the axis of moisture advection around the north and west side of the low.  Eight to tweleve inches of snow was reported to have fallen in association with this band, while 3 to four inch amounts were common to the north and south.  Gusty fifteen to twenty five mile per hour winds exacerbated the reduced visibilities within the heavy snow region.  Accidents were reported across the warning area, but none were serious, with mainly slid-offs reported.  Schools across northeast Kansas were closed as a result of the heavy snowfall, including the University of Kansas. One of the main concerns during this busy winter for several cities was the availabilty of road salt.  Many cities were nearly out of salt by the time this snow storm hit, forcing officials to use a mix of salt and sand on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought 5-6 inches of snow to parts of the Tri-State area, accompanied by winds of 40-50 mph.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in jack-knifed trailers and several vehicle accidents. Some state and county roads were closed for a short period of time due to minor accidents, drifting and reduced visibility in falling and blowing snow. In Kansas, snow totals were:  Cheyenne county 5-6 inches, Rawlins county 4-7 inches, and in Sherman county 4-5 inches. Peak winds reached 51 mph in Sherman county and were generally around 45 mph elsewhere for much of the day.

A potent winter storm moved into western Kansas during the early morning of Tuesday, February 5th and then marched eastward through central Kansas during the day Tuesday.  As the upper low tracked west to east through Kansas, a surface low pressure system moved slowly east along the Kansas-Oklahoma border.  This combination resulted in moderate to heavy snow in parts of western and central Kansas.  Before the snow ended, from 6 to 8 inches fell in a 30 to 40 mile-wide swath from Hays to Kalvesta (in Finney County).  An area of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell south of this heavier band, and was basically north of a line from Johnson to Jetmore to east of La Crosse in Rush County.  One to two inches of snow fell to the south of this mentioned area, with a few patches of 3-plus inches of snow.  This 1 to 2-inch band was north of a line from Elkhart to Dodge City to Greensburg to south of St. John.  Considerable blowing and drifting of snow was reported, as north winds blew at 20 to 30 mph during the moderate to heavy snow.  In Gray County just west of Cimarron during the mid-morning of Tuesday, freezing drizzle and fog formed a nearly solid sheet of ice along US Highway 50 along a curve and a hill.  Blowing snow worsened the visibility in that area, and numerous accidents occurred.  Several cars slid off the road, and 2 power poles were downed.  However, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low level jet during the morning hours of February 21st. The more robust cells that developed were prolific precipitation producers. The 850mb temperatures were above freezing across Anderson and Coffey counties, while 925mb temperatures were two to eight degrees below freezing.  Sleet was the dominant precipitation type in these counties.  Two inches of sleet accumulated in Coffey county, while one half to one and a half inch accumulations were common across the rest of the county, as well as Anderson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain, sleet, and snow fell for a short period of time during the late morning and early afternoon of the 21st.  Seven tenths of an inch of frozen precipitation, the mix of sleet and snow, was measured at the NWS-Topeka office.  Minor vehicle accidents were reported across the county.  Only one injury was reported near Topeka High school when a vehicle failed to stop and slid through a cross walk as a result of slick road conditions.  The vehicle struck a pedestrian attempting to cross the road.

Approximately one quarter of an inch of sleet fell before noon.  The next two hours saw approximately half of an inch of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An interesting weather event took place the evening of the 26th through the morning hours of the 27th as an intense shortwave trough swung through the central plains states. Strong warm advection ahead of the upper trough and modest elevated instability allowed scattered thunderstorms to develop, mainly across northeast and east central Kansas. A few reports of hail and strong winds were relayed to the NWS.  A strong surface cold front brought sub-freezing surface temperatures to the region quickly during the early morning hours. This shallow freezing layer and persistent warm advection aloft set the stage for a period of freezing rain and between one quarter and one half inch of ice accumulation, primarily across Jackson and Jefferson Counties. Light snow later in the afternoon brought a dusting of snow to up to around half of an inch for portions of northeast and east central Kansas. Several accidents were reported due to relatively heavy traffic flow after the Christmas holiday, but no injuries or fatalities occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported up to half of an inch of accumulated ice in Osage County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts up to 60 mph occurred across southeast Kansas into west central Missouri as a powerful low pressure system tracked from the central plains to the Great Lakes.  These high winds downed trees and power lines as well as causing some roof damage across extreme southeast Kansas and west central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to 60 mph.  Power outages reported in Columbus. Pieces of siding blown from homes.  Large playground equipment rolled over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through Kansas late in the evening of the 23rd through the morning hours of the 24th. A brief shot of light snow and freezing drizzle accompanied the frontal passage. Several accidents were reported to have occurred on icy roadways, and the counties that offered injury information have been included in this episode. One half of an inch to around two and a half inches of snow was common across the local area, with a glaze of ice mixed into the snow accumulation layer and particularly evident on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accidents were reported across Geary County the morning of the 24th. One man was airlifted to a local hospital in critical condition after his truck slid off a slick I-70 near Junction City and hit a tree. One to two inches of snow was reported to have fallen across the county. A period of light freezing rain also left a glaze of ice on roadways before the snow fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three accidents were reported in the city of Ottawa, one of which involved three vehicles and left one individual injured.  Three other accidents were reported elsewhere in the county.  A glaze of ice and around one inch of snow were reported to have accumulated across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isentropic ascent coupled with forcing aloft in the form of the left exit quadrant of an upper level jet streak offered large scale lift across the forecast area, and helped to produce a widespread snowfall event across the local area. Snow totals were generally in the one to three inch range, although a handful of cooperative observers north of an Ottawa to Jackson County line offered accumulation reports up to four or five inches. Numerous vehicle accidents were reported, with only a few minor injuries included.  Specific counties mentioned within this episode were those that offered an accident and injury count.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to nearly three an a half inches of snow was reported to have fallen across the county. Several accidents were reported by law enforcement to have occurred across the county. One woman was transported to the hospital after her vehicle struck a parked semi just before 11am CST. The majority of the seven accidents reported on the 16th actually occurred after the snow ceased falling around the noon CST hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate accidents resulted in the injury of two individuals in Wabaunsee County on the 16th. Three to four inches of snow was reported to have fallen across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighty-nine traffic accidents were reported in the Topeka area on the 16th as a result of the slick road conditions. A portion of the turnpike between Wanamaker and 21st Street in Topeka was closed for a portion of the morning.  Further east on the turnpike, three accidents were reported to the west of the Lecompton exit ramp, one of which involved eight vehicles.  Five injuries were reported.   Another accident in the early afternoon resulted in two injuries. Between one and three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-topped convection in a highly sheared environment brought a narrow path of straight line wind damage from South Central Coffey County beginning around 11:45 pm on January 7th and extending Northeast through West Central and Central Anderson County through approximately 12:40 am on January 8th.  Damage was done to trees and power poles and a couple of outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately eight six-line powerlines snapped of a quarter of the way from the top.  The powerlines were blown eastward onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial shortwave trough spurred a cold front to dive through the region late the evening of the 8th through the morning of the 9th.  Warm advection rain showers changed over to snow as the system passed through, with between one and three inches common over portions of east central Kansas. Dozens of accidents occurred along the roadways of several counties.  One vehicle crash fatality was attributed to the weather, as were numerous reported injuries that occurred as a result of wrecks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous accidents were reported across Franklin County, specifically around the Ottawa area. Two of the more serious accidents occurred when a truck jack-knifed along I-35 and a semi overturned on Hwy 59.  Several slide-offs occurred in conjunction with the jack-knifed truck. Numerous other accidents occurred in and around Ottawa. There were no injuries. Approximately two inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite estimated ice accumulations from a slick glaze up to one quarter of an inch, residents of northeast Kansas were left relatively unscathed by freezing rain and freezing drizzle on the 18th.  Local law enforcement officials were quoted in the Topeka Capital Journal inferring that motorists simply heeded weather advisories and stayed home.  Traffic across the northeast corner of the state was noticeably light during the event, but unfortunately, several accidents still occurred.  Two injuries were reported in Shawnee County, and one in Wabaunsee County because of the slick conditions on bridges and overpasses.  Wet, slushy, and occasionally icy roadways were common during the morning hours elsewhere across the county warning area. Specific counties noted within this event were where injury accidents had been reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven vehicle accidents caused by icy roadways were reported in Shawnee County. Two injuries were noted by local law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air in concert with an upper level disturbance, interacting with an arctic airmass over the region resulted in moderate to heavy sleet  with thunder and lightning across south-central and southeast Kansas during the early morning hours of February 21st. The sleet only lasted a few hours, but accumulated to a depth of 3 inches in Winfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of light freezing drizzle and flurries likely contributed to a fatal car accident (indirect) around 1:50 a.m. in Hesston on February 21st. A 24-year-old Goessel man was killed in the 500 block of South Ridge Road in Hesston when his vehicle slid off the road and struck a tree. He was pronounced dead at the scene. Ice and snow accumulations were very minor, but enough to create slick conditions. Information courtesy The Hutchinson News.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system produced widespread northwest winds of 60-70 mph across the Tri-State region.  Light snowfall combined with the extreme wind, resulting in blizzard conditions in some areas. Some of the stronger county wind reports include: 62 mph in Cheyenne, 59 mph in Rawlins, 78 mph in Sherman, 58 mph in Thomas, 59 mph in Wallace, 63 mph in Logan, 58 mph in Gove, 61 mph in Greeley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 50 to 65 mph occurred across all of NW Kansas for much of the day.  A wind of 62 mph was measured near Bird City at 936 am CDT.
