EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The low level jet increased during the early morning hours and impinged upon a stationary boundary draped from northeast Kansas southwest into central Kansas.  One strong thunderstorm produced some nickle to quarter sized hail.

Two rounds of severe weather defined the evening hours of April 10th.  Discrete thunderstorms developed out ahead of a surface front during the late afternoon.  Large hail, up to one inch in diameter, was the primary reported event with this convection. Convection redeveloped across east central Kansas during the evening, into a line segment that lifted to the northeast. This line of storms produced strong, damaging winds with estimated wind speeds up to 80mph. Damage mainly impacted tree branches and powerlines.  But, several reports of building damage came in as well, and one tractor trailer was blown off the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles blown off the apartment complex of a NWS-Employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across north-central Kansas and produced large hail, strong winds and heavy rains. Hail up to the size of softballs was reported west of Stockton where windows were broken, roofs and siding damaged and crops pummeled.  A couple of brief tornados were reported to have touched down in Rooks County.  One was south of Stockton and the other was near Woodston. No damage occurred with these.  There were other reports of hail, mainly up to the size of golf balls, and wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph reported across the area.  A tornado touched down north of Beloit in Mitchell County and traveled east. Several houses, outbuildings, grain bins, trees and power poles were damaged along the it���s path. This tornado crossed into Cloud County.

Synoptically, a large upper trough extended across the northern and central Rockies.  An upper level shortwave ejected across the southern plains off of the larger scale trough, and interacted with a moist and unstable airmass east of a cold front.  The cold front was positioned along the Interstate 35 corridor when storms developed.  These storms began to impact extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks during the early morning hours of 22 April.  Mainly large hail and localized flooding were the primary hazards with this activity.  Other storms redeveloped during the afternoon and evening hours of 22 April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed large trees.

Excessive rainfall developed over southern Missouri during the evening of 17 March. A line of training convection assumed a position roughly along a line from Anderson to Ozark to Licking. This convection expanded with time, eventually covering all of Crawford and Cherokee counties. Moderate to heavy rain continued into the overnight period and did not stop until the morning of 19 March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three and a half inches of rain fell over Cherokee County.  Widespread flooding along creeks and mainstem rivers occurred.  The Spring River experienced moderate flooding while Cherry and Lightning creeks flooded lowlands along these streams.  Numerous county roads flooded.

Synoptically, a large upper trough extended across the northern and central Rockies.  An upper level shortwave ejected across the southern plains off of the larger scale trough, and interacted with a moist and unstable airmass east of a cold front.  The cold front was positioned along the Interstate 35 corridor when storms developed.  These storms began to impact extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks during the early morning hours of 22 April.  Mainly large hail and localized flooding were the primary hazards with this activity.  Other storms redeveloped during the afternoon and evening hours of 22 April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated development of storms along and north of an advancing warm front led to a large swath of greater than three inches of rain over extreme southeast Kansas.  This excessive rain occurred on wet soil conditions from record rainfall in February and March.  Due to an unusually high water table along with saturated soil conditions, all counties within the NWS Springfield County Warning Area experienced widespread flooding on nearly all secondary roads.  Emergency Management Directors across the region that were responsible for baracading roads, stated that as little as one half of an inch of rain was causing widespread road closures across their county of interest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell over Crawford County.  Nearly all low lying areas that typically flood during periods of excessive rainfall were flooded.  Lightning Creek and Cowskin Creek were impacted the greatest.  A water rescue was performed along Cowskin Creek south of Pittsburg.

A potent early Spring storm system brought severe weather to South Central Kansas the evening of April 7th into the early morning hours of April 8th. A few storms brought small hail to parts of Southeast Kansas during the day but it was a series intense bow echoes that caused extensive straight-line wind damage Sedgwick, Harvey and especially Butler and Greenwood Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense bow echo moved across northern Butler county producing widespread wind damage from the Potwin area through Cassoday. Seventy four very large high voltage H-poles were downed along with 130 power poles and a 300 ft cell phone tower also collapsed. Additionally, 3 semi trucks were blown off of the Kansas Turnpike which resulted in one injury. Several calves were also killed by flying debris near Cassoday.

A strong 500mb storm system dug into the four corners region the morning of February 5th, while a surface low established itself over northwest Texas.  A cold front extended from the surface low northeastward. Southerly midlevel winds overrode this boundary, and precipitation developed through the day of the 5th. Cold advection north of the front also brought snow to portions of north central Kansas during the morning. The precipitation type along and south of the boundary in the morning was rain, with a few embedded thunderstorms. There was a brief lull in the precipitation when the low level jet slackened during the late morning. The upper and surface lows lifted to the northeast during the early afternoon, and the cold front established itself along a southwest to northeast line nearly bisecting the local area. Temperatures north of the boundary hovered right around to a few degrees below freezing, while temperatures south of the front were above the freezing mark near the surface and aloft. The front gradually slipped eastward through the day, allowing temperatures to gradually drop below freezing.  The precipitation type switched over to all snow by mid afternoon.  A band of heavy snow established itself from near Brown County to near Morris County for the remainder of the day, and through the night within the axis of moisture advection around the north and west side of the low.  Eight to tweleve inches of snow was reported to have fallen in association with this band, while 3 to four inch amounts were common to the north and south.  Gusty fifteen to twenty five mile per hour winds exacerbated the reduced visibilities within the heavy snow region.  Accidents were reported across the warning area, but none were serious, with mainly slid-offs reported.  Schools across northeast Kansas were closed as a result of the heavy snowfall, including the University of Kansas. One of the main concerns during this busy winter for several cities was the availabilty of road salt.  Many cities were nearly out of salt by the time this snow storm hit, forcing officials to use a mix of salt and sand on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow was reported to have fallen 4 miles southeast of Baileyville, with eight inches of snow was in the city of Seneca. Six to nine inches of snow were common across the rest of the county. Significant blowing and drifting of the snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system produced widespread northwest winds of 60-70 mph across the Tri-State region.  Light snowfall combined with the extreme wind, resulting in blizzard conditions in some areas. Some of the stronger county wind reports include: 62 mph in Cheyenne, 59 mph in Rawlins, 78 mph in Sherman, 58 mph in Thomas, 59 mph in Wallace, 63 mph in Logan, 58 mph in Gove, 61 mph in Greeley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 50 to 65 mph occurred across all of NW Kansas for much of the day. In Sherman county, a peak wind of 78 mph was measured 12 miles west of Goodland at 905 am MDT. A traffic accident occurred at about this time on Interstate 70 west of Goodland when strong winds suddenly reduced visibility to near zero in blowing dirt.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 50 to 65 mph occurred across all of NW Kansas for much of the day. Minor damage occurred to a roof in Colby, and 4-6 inch diameter live tree branches were broken due to the high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 50 to 65 mph occurred across all of NW Kansas for much of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe weather defined the evening hours of April 10th.  Discrete thunderstorms developed out ahead of a surface front during the late afternoon.  Large hail, up to one inch in diameter, was the primary reported event with this convection. Convection redeveloped across east central Kansas during the evening, into a line segment that lifted to the northeast. This line of storms produced strong, damaging winds with estimated wind speeds up to 80mph. Damage mainly impacted tree branches and powerlines.  But, several reports of building damage came in as well, and one tractor trailer was blown off the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles blown off roof of spotter's home, and the homes of several neighbors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms tracked across southeast Kansas early the morning of the 31st.  A strong, 55kt to 65kt low level jet helped to fuel these thunderstorms. An anvil shield overspread the skies of east central Kanas. Light rain from the anvil fell through the strong low level jet and forced this newly cooled air to rapidly push down to the surface.  The momentum transport brought a period of high winds to east central Kansas, with reports up to 70mph and damage reported.  The most significant damage occurred to the Lyndon High School, where the roof was torn off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One mile east of the city of Pomona, estimated 65 to 70 mph winds blew over a car port and knocked down several power poles.  Two Pomona area residents also reported that damage was done to livestock buildings.

Two rounds of severe weather defined the evening hours of April 10th.  Discrete thunderstorms developed out ahead of a surface front during the late afternoon.  Large hail, up to one inch in diameter, was the primary reported event with this convection. Convection redeveloped across east central Kansas during the evening, into a line segment that lifted to the northeast. This line of storms produced strong, damaging winds with estimated wind speeds up to 80mph. Damage mainly impacted tree branches and powerlines.  But, several reports of building damage came in as well, and one tractor trailer was blown off the roadway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five buildings at the Emporia Livestock sales were damaged when severe thunderstorm winds blew across Emporia. The roofs were torn from three sheds, and the debris was tossed several streets away and onto powerlines, which caused sparks. The power was out to several local businesses and residences. Country Mart North also sustained damage to the sign on the front of it's building. A piece of the sign flew across the street and knocked over a portion of fence. Several trees and power lines were also reported down along the damage path.  Sheds and local business signs in addition to those above were also reported to have been damaged. Seven hundred and seven people were reported to have lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought a cold front through eastern half of Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 1st. The front was the trigger for initial severe thunderstorms in Southeast Kansas. These storms produced several short-lived tornadoes as well as large hail and damaging winds. Another line of severe storms quickly developed along the mid-level front over South Central Kansas. This line of storms moved to east across all of Southeast Kansas but its intensity weakened as it crossed the Flint Hills. The second round of storms produced large, high winds and very heavy rain.

A cluster of thunderstorms impacted Cherokee County, Kansas during the early morning hours of 2 May, 2008.  Damaging wind was the primary hazard with this episode.

Two clusters of showers and thunderstorms impacted mainly extreme southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri. Flash flooding occurred with both clusters. Two to six inches of rain fell on saturated soil conditions, leading to a quick onset of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of rain fell over Crawford County.  Nearly all locations that experiences flooding during periods of excessive rainfall were flooded.  The area that was impacted the greatest was Cowskin Creek.  Numerous areas near the city of Pittsburg were flooded including Lincoln Park and a mobile home park near Quincy and Lonestar Streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of rain fell over Cherokee County.  All areas that typically flood during periods of excessive rain were flooded.  A few specific locations included the intersection of 110th Street and Scammon Road, a few thousand acres near the confluence of Lightning Creek and the Neosho River, and several streets in the city of Columbus.

A series of intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the area during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Long-track tornadoes, flash flooding, large hail and damaging winds were reported across much of northwest Kansas into extreme southern Nebraska.  Eleven tornadoes were verified by storm chasers and damage surveys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported running over a county road 4 miles south of Grinnell.    Other areas of eastern Gove county also experienced extremely heavy rainfall through early evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding with water up to 1 foot deep was reported in the city of Norton via the National Spotter Network. Local newspapers later stated that Norton county roads incurred an estimated $500,000 in damage as a result of the heavy rainfall and flooding in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front surged into warm moist air across South Central Kansas the afternoon of March 2nd, spawning severe thunderstorms. The line of thunderstorms erupted on the cold front and produced damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph, however, a channeled microburst of 100 mph occurred in Cowley County.  Golf ball sized hail and even an EF0 tornado occurred with this event.

Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly during the early morning hours of May 31st across south-central Kansas, producing very large hail and damaging winds. Strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of mainly central and south-central Kansas from the late evening hours of the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. A strong and deep upper level disturbance and associated sharp dryline sparked supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon hours of the 23rd across western portions of Kansas and Oklahoma. These supercell thunderstorms moved east to northeast into portions of central and south-central Kansas during the evening hours, producing tornadoes, damaging straight-line winds and large hail. Ahead of these supercells, very strong south to southeast winds up to 60 mph developed for a time across central Kansas. By late evening, a squall line overtook the supercell thunderstorms over south-central Kansas, producing localized damaging straight-line winds into the early morning hours of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down briefly in open country. Minor damage occurred to several trees, and 5 power poles were snapped.

Another severe weather episode moved across the forecast area producing widespread flooding and hail across most of the counties served.  A few high wind events were also noted.  Several high water rescues were noted during this time frame as people would drive into water covered roads and their vehicle would become inundated with water before finally retiring.  This caused for some consternation with local law and fire officials as they had to rescue the individuals from on top of their vehicle or in one instance they were clinging to a tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by KAKE channel 10.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first signs of strong wind damage along this path were noted at a farm on E 250 Road. The gusts then pushed into the city of Lawrence. Damage was reported across Lawrence as a result of the severe thunderstorm winds. Large tree limbs were reported down on the north side of the city. Several businesses lost their signs or awnings, and received minor damage to the building. Power flashes were noted by a motorist driving along Interstate 70 near mile marker 201. A spotter had been driving along 6th Street near Wakarusa when strong winds overtook his car and forced him to pull over.  He estimated wind speeds around 80 miles per hour, and possibly stronger. One transformer was blown near this location. Power outages across the city affected 8400 residences. A deck roof was blown over a house at another residence. A mobile home on First Street in North Lawrence was heavily damaged when a tree fell onto the bedroom portion of the trailer. Eighteen to twenty inch diameter trees were blown down at the intersections of 6th Street and Queens Road, and at 6th Street and Iowa. A tractor trailer was also reported to have been blown over in north Lawrence.  At least one tree fell onto a home and caused damage. The Langston Hughes School gymnasium on the west side of town sustained approximately eighty thousand dollars in roof damage.

Strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of Central and South-Central Kansas from the late evening hours of May 22nd through the early morning hours of May 23rd. Thunderstorms initiated along a dryline during the late afternoon hours of May 22nd over the Texas Panhandle and Western Oklahoma, and eventually moved east into Central and South-Central Kansas. Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly during the early morning hours of May 31st across south-central Kansas, producing very large hail and damaging winds. Hail as large softballs pummeled portions of Cowley county, with other numerous hail reports reaching golfball and baseball size across the area. Residence and car windows were cracked and shattered, commercial and residential properties sustained roof damage, and numerous vehicles sustained hail damage. The communities of Floral, Burden, Dexter and New Salem, all in Cowley County, were hardest hit. Unfortunately, an estimate on the total amount of damage inflicted is unknown. The storms weakened as they moved east to southeast into southeast Kansas, producing marginally severe hail. Additionally, areas of relatively minor flooding occurred due to numerous thunderstorms moving over the same areas.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Western Kansas and eventually morphed into a bow echo that moved through parts of Central Kansas. This cluster of storms eventually was overtaken by another severe line of storms that moved through South Central Kansas. Although there were a few hail reports the biggest hazard from these storms were severe straight-line winds. The remnants from these storms moved into Southeast Kansas causing minor flooding.

Thunderstorms developed along a dryline during the late afternoon of May 1st, and sustained as the synoptic cold front dove through the region later that evening. Two tornadoes touched down and caused damage in portions of Osage and Douglas Counties.  One residence in Douglas County was almost completely demolished by one of the tornadoes, rated an EF-2. Hail from the size of quarters to the size of golfballs was also reported across portions of northeast and east central Kansas. After 11pm CST, the severe weather threat focus turned to strong winds. Trees were damaged across portions of the warning area, as was property including homes, outbuidings, power poles, and transformers. The Emergency Manager from Osage County reported that property damage estimates were around $100,000. Spotters estimated wind speeds up to 70 mph, and a measured gust of 71 mph was reported by the ASOS at the Lawrence Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metal building that houses the Lecompton Township Fire Department had it's north and south doors blown in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low level jet overnight the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. The extremely moist airmass in place lead to efficiant rainfall producing thunderstorms. Local Doppler Radar rainfall estimates showed that four to six inches of rain had fallen across portions of Jackson, Jefferson, and Nemaha counties during the overnight hours. One report from a spotter in Whiting noted that eight inches of precipitation had fallen through the night. The heavy rain and associated run-off caused problems across these counties, from water flowing over roadways to filled drainage ditches and crop fields. At least one bridge was damaged by the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported to be running over roadways across portions of the county. M Road was littered with debris as a result of water flowing over the road. Several raods were deemed impassable.  Portions of N Road sunk or developed large holes. The backwall of a bridge collapsed near the intersection of 246 and N Roads. Rainfall reports near the roadways most significantly affected by the heavy rainfall included eight inches of rain in the Whiting area, and four inches to the north of Holton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into north central Kansas early the morning of the 24th. Hail to the size of half dollars fell, and winds to 70 mph were reported across the region. Minor damage was reported, mainly to tree limbs, power lines, and some structures. One home had it's roof partially torn off, and it's carport destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four men were camping at Pomona Lake State Park. A thunderstorm rolled through, and lightning struck the tent in which the men were sleeping. The three men in the tent were injured, but the fourth man standing outside the tent near a tree was killed. It was reported that he was killed as he headed toward more sturdy shelter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large upper level trough over the western U.S. continued to eject energy out over the Central Plains, with a surface low remaining anchored over eastern Colorado with a stationary boundary draped east through the region. Storms once again initially developed during the late afternoon near the dry line over western Kansas and drifted east; with the increase of the low level jet helping storms which had developed along the stationary front continue on well into the late evening hours. In north-central Kansas, storms produced hail mainly one inch in diameter. Baseball sized hail was reported south of Zurick in southern Rooks County. A couple of brief tornadoes sat down in Rooks County. One tornado occurred just to the north of the Ellis/Rooks County line. This tornado snapped several power poles, damaged trees and an outbuilding.  The second tornado started near Plainville and lifted just north of Codell. Several power poles were downed as well as trees damaged. It was another night of efficient storms in regard to rainfall, dropping several inches over portions of north-central Kansas, causing additional problems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of mainly central and south-central Kansas from the late evening hours of the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. A strong and deep upper level disturbance and associated sharp dryline sparked supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon hours of the 23rd across western portions of Kansas and Oklahoma. These supercell thunderstorms moved east to northeast into portions of central and south-central Kansas during the evening hours, producing tornadoes, damaging straight-line winds and large hail. Ahead of these supercells, very strong south to southeast winds up to 60 mph developed for a time across central Kansas. By late evening, a squall line overtook the supercell thunderstorms over south-central Kansas, producing localized damaging straight-line winds into the early morning hours of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The estimated wind gust was observed at the west gate at Cheney Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passing through the Central Plains provided a focus for strong to severe thunderstorm development during the evening hours on May 6th. Thunderstorms forming along this front developed into a squall line, which had a slow southeast progression. These storms produced winds up to 60 MPH.

A slow moving upper level low pressure system worked across the Central Plains during the first couple of days of May.  As it moved east, a surface low pressure system and cold front were pushed across the region, aiding in the development of thunderstorms across north central Kansas.

Strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of mainly central and south-central Kansas from the late evening hours of the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. A strong and deep upper level disturbance and associated sharp dryline sparked supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon hours of the 23rd across western portions of Kansas and Oklahoma. These supercell thunderstorms moved east to northeast into portions of central and south-central Kansas during the evening hours, producing tornadoes, damaging straight-line winds and large hail. Ahead of these supercells, very strong south to southeast winds up to 60 mph developed for a time across central Kansas. By late evening, a squall line overtook the supercell thunderstorms over south-central Kansas, producing localized damaging straight-line winds into the early morning hours of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread minor tree damage was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a dryline during the late afternoon of May 1st, and sustained as the synoptic cold front dove through the region later that evening. Two tornadoes touched down and caused damage in portions of Osage and Douglas Counties.  One residence in Douglas County was almost completely demolished by one of the tornadoes, rated an EF-2. Hail from the size of quarters to the size of golfballs was also reported across portions of northeast and east central Kansas. After 11pm CST, the severe weather threat focus turned to strong winds. Trees were damaged across portions of the warning area, as was property including homes, outbuidings, power poles, and transformers. The Emergency Manager from Osage County reported that property damage estimates were around $100,000. Spotters estimated wind speeds up to 70 mph, and a measured gust of 71 mph was reported by the ASOS at the Lawrence Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust speed around 40 miles per hour also reported.

Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly during the early morning hours of May 31st across south-central Kansas, producing very large hail and damaging winds. Hail as large softballs pummeled portions of Cowley county, with other numerous hail reports reaching golfball and baseball size across the area. Residence and car windows were cracked and shattered, commercial and residential properties sustained roof damage, and numerous vehicles sustained hail damage. The communities of Floral, Burden, Dexter and New Salem, all in Cowley County, were hardest hit. Unfortunately, an estimate on the total amount of damage inflicted is unknown. The storms weakened as they moved east to southeast into southeast Kansas, producing marginally severe hail. Additionally, areas of relatively minor flooding occurred due to numerous thunderstorms moving over the same areas.

Strong to severe thunderstorms affected portions of Central and South-Central Kansas from the late evening hours of May 22nd through the early morning hours of May 23rd. Thunderstorms initiated along a dryline during the late afternoon hours of May 22nd over the Texas Panhandle and Western Oklahoma, and eventually moved east into Central and South-Central Kansas. These thunderstorms, along with additional redevelopment, produced up to 70 mph winds, golfball size hail and very heavy, and localized rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over northern Oklahoma during the late evening hours of May 22nd produced 50 to 60 mph southerly winds across Sedgwick county. A 62 mph wind gust was measured at McConnell AFB.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Western Kansas and eventually morphed into a bow echo that moved through parts of Central Kansas. This cluster of storms eventually was overtaken by another severe line of storms that moved through South Central Kansas. Although there were a few hail reports the biggest hazard from these storms were severe straight-line winds. The remnants from these storms moved into Southeast Kansas causing minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another severe weather episode moved across the forecast area producing widespread flooding and hail across most of the counties served.  A few high wind events were also noted.  Several high water rescues were noted during this time frame as people would drive into water covered roads and their vehicle would become inundated with water before finally retiring.  This caused for some consternation with local law and fire officials as they had to rescue the individuals from on top of their vehicle or in one instance they were clinging to a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low level jet overnight the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. The extremely moist airmass in place lead to efficiant rainfall producing thunderstorms. Local Doppler Radar rainfall estimates showed that four to six inches of rain had fallen across portions of Jackson, Jefferson, and Nemaha counties during the overnight hours. One report from a spotter in Whiting noted that eight inches of precipitation had fallen through the night. The heavy rain and associated run-off caused problems across these counties, from water flowing over roadways to filled drainage ditches and crop fields. At least one bridge was damaged by the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 9 was closed between Goff and Wetmore as a result of high water over the roadway. Highway 187 north of Centralia was also closed due to water over the roadway. A tributary of the Black Vermillion River south of Baileyville rose out of it's banks, flooding fields and flowing onto several local roadways. A secondary roadway was covered with flash flood waters. A newspaper reported that the Nemaha River rose quickly and nearly crested over top of the cement weir in place near the former Lake Namaha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed during the mid-afternoon hours and continued through early evening. Hail and minor flooding occurred in eastern Colorado and southwest Nebraska. In northwest Kansas, damaging straight-line thunderstorm winds broke utility poles, downed trees, and destroyed a garage and carport near Sharon Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A feed yard northwest of Leoti, KS suffered damage from strong thunderstorm outflows. About 20% of the feed warehouse roof was removed.  Two smaller wood-frame metal-skin sheds were heavily damaged. Four by eight foot hay bales were blown a short distance. Three irrigation pivots just south of the feed yard were overturned. All debris was blown from SSW to NNE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large upper level trough over the western U.S. continued to eject energy out over the Central Plains, with a surface low remaining anchored over eastern Colorado with a stationary boundary draped east through the region. Storms once again initially developed during the late afternoon near the dry line over western Kansas and drifted east; with the increase of the low level jet helping storms which had developed along the stationary front continue on well into the late evening hours. In north-central Kansas, storms produced hail mainly one inch in diameter. Baseball sized hail was reported south of Zurick in southern Rooks County. A couple of brief tornadoes sat down in Rooks County. One tornado occurred just to the north of the Ellis/Rooks County line. This tornado snapped several power poles, damaged trees and an outbuilding.  The second tornado started near Plainville and lifted just north of Codell. Several power poles were downed as well as trees damaged. It was another night of efficient storms in regard to rainfall, dropping several inches over portions of north-central Kansas, causing additional problems.

Another severe weather episode moved across the forecast area producing widespread flooding and hail across most of the counties served.  A few high wind events were also noted.  Several high water rescues were noted during this time frame as people would drive into water covered roads and their vehicle would become inundated with water before finally retiring.  This caused for some consternation with local law and fire officials as they had to rescue the individuals from on top of their vehicle or in one instance they were clinging to a tree.

Intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the region during the afternoon and evening hours producing 10 tornadoes, including an F4 tornado just SW of Quinter. Extremely heavy rainfall also resulted in flooding in many areas. Many of these thunderstorms occurred in the same areas as the storms the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large wedge tornado developed about 9 miles SSW of Quinter and moved north, then northwest, eventually dissipating 2 miles SE of Park. This tornado caused extensive damage to several homes, including EF4 damage to one home which was completely destroyed. A car and a pickup truck were tossed or bounced 200 yards at the residence. Nearby, two other homes suffered extensive damage. This tornado caused two injuries, including the driver of a vehicle which was thrown off Interstate 70, and an older male attempting to get into his storm cellar when he was struck by flying debris.

Large upper level trough over the western U.S. continued to eject energy out over the Central Plains, with a surface low remaining anchored over eastern Colorado with a stationary boundary draped east through the region. Storms once again initially developed during the late afternoon near the dry line over western Kansas and drifted east; with the increase of the low level jet helping storms which had developed along the stationary front continue on well into the late evening hours. In north-central Kansas, storms produced hail mainly one inch in diameter. Baseball sized hail was reported south of Zurick in southern Rooks County. A couple of brief tornadoes sat down in Rooks County. One tornado occurred just to the north of the Ellis/Rooks County line. This tornado snapped several power poles, damaged trees and an outbuilding.  The second tornado started near Plainville and lifted just north of Codell. Several power poles were downed as well as trees damaged. It was another night of efficient storms in regard to rainfall, dropping several inches over portions of north-central Kansas, causing additional problems.

A large upper level trough located over the western U.S. was ejecting pieces of energy out over the Central Plains. Ahead of this trough at the surface, low pressure developed over eastern Colorado, helping to push a warm front north into the forecast area. The combination of these weather systems brought thunderstorms to north-central Kansas. Storms first initiated over western Kansas along the dry line, with more development later on along the warm front. Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours and continued on well into the evening. This event was also the first in a series of nights in which many areas saw heavy rain.

Long-track tornadoes, flash flooding, large hail and damaging winds were reported across much of northwest Kansas into extreme southern Nebraska.  Eleven tornadoes were verified by storm chasers and damage surveys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved across rural eastern Gove county during the late afternoon and produced EF1 damage at a home on Road O before dissipating. Severe roof damage occurred and several outbuildings were damaged or destroyed.

By late evening, a squall line overtook the supercell thunderstorms over south-central Kansas, producing localized damaging straight-line winds into the early morning hours of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was pushed off of its foundation, and a barn was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a dryline during the late afternoon of May 1st, and sustained as the synoptic cold front dove through the region later that evening. Two tornadoes touched down and caused damage in portions of Osage and Douglas Counties.  One residence in Douglas County was almost completely demolished by one of the tornadoes, rated an EF-2. Hail from the size of quarters to the size of golfballs was also reported across portions of northeast and east central Kansas. After 11pm CST, the severe weather threat focus turned to strong winds. Trees were damaged across portions of the warning area, as was property including homes, outbuidings, power poles, and transformers. The Emergency Manager from Osage County reported that property damage estimates were around $100,000. Spotters estimated wind speeds up to 70 mph, and a measured gust of 71 mph was reported by the ASOS at the Lawrence Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second swath of golf ball size hail was reported along this path by several spotters. Hail with this storm was more sporadic, but larger than with the previous cell.

Another severe weather episode moved across the forecast area producing widespread flooding and hail across most of the counties served.  A few high wind events were also noted.  Several high water rescues were noted during this time frame as people would drive into water covered roads and their vehicle would become inundated with water before finally retiring.  This caused for some consternation with local law and fire officials as they had to rescue the individuals from on top of their vehicle or in one instance they were clinging to a tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Highway 166 near 11th street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large upper level trough over the western U.S. continued to eject energy out over the Central Plains, with a surface low remaining anchored over eastern Colorado with a stationary boundary draped east through the region. Storms once again initially developed during the late afternoon near the dry line over western Kansas and drifted east; with the increase of the low level jet helping storms which had developed along the stationary front continue on well into the late evening hours. In north-central Kansas, storms produced hail mainly one inch in diameter. Baseball sized hail was reported south of Zurick in southern Rooks County. A couple of brief tornadoes sat down in Rooks County. One tornado occurred just to the north of the Ellis/Rooks County line. This tornado snapped several power poles, damaged trees and an outbuilding.  The second tornado started near Plainville and lifted just north of Codell. Several power poles were downed as well as trees damaged. It was another night of efficient storms in regard to rainfall, dropping several inches over portions of north-central Kansas, causing additional problems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly during the early morning hours of May 31st across south-central Kansas, producing very large hail and damaging winds. Hail as large softballs pummeled portions of Cowley county, with other numerous hail reports reaching golfball and baseball size across the area. Residence and car windows were cracked and shattered, commercial and residential properties sustained roof damage, and numerous vehicles sustained hail damage. The communities of Floral, Burden, Dexter and New Salem, all in Cowley County, were hardest hit. Unfortunately, an estimate on the total amount of damage inflicted is unknown. The storms weakened as they moved east to southeast into southeast Kansas, producing marginally severe hail. Additionally, areas of relatively minor flooding occurred due to numerous thunderstorms moving over the same areas.

Elevated convection developed on the nose of the low level jet late the evening of the 24th, and continued through the morning of the 25th. Severe hail was the primary threat with the elevated storms, but a lone, long track supercell the developed over the northwestern part of the state was able to sustain it's strength through Cloud County. A relatively long track tornado crossed through Jewell County to just south of Jamestown in Cloud County before lifting. EF-1 damage was sustained in the Topeka Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 60 mph gust on home weather station.

An upper level disturbance set off isolated severe thunderstorms across portions of East Central Kansas.  A downburst that produced severe winds and sporadic wind damage and hail around 1 inch in diameter highlight the episode.

A potent upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary sparked strong to severe thunderstorms from the mid morning through the afternoon hours of April 23rd across central, south-central and southeast Kansas. Hail as large as golfballs and winds up to 60 mph were reported. Additionally, slow moving and training thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall amounts, particularly across the northern half of Marion County, where excessive runoff caused the North Cottonwood River to overflow its banks, flooding the town of Durham with around 18 inches of water.

A potent upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary sparked strong to severe thunderstorms from the mid morning through the afternoon hours of April 23rd across central, south-central and southeast Kansas. Hail as large as golfballs and winds up to 60 mph were reported. Additionally, slow moving and training thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall amounts, particularly across the northern half of Marion County, where excessive runoff caused the North Cottonwood River to overflow its banks, flooding the town of Durham with around 18 inches of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of penny size hail occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly during the early morning hours of May 31st across south-central Kansas, producing very large hail and damaging winds. Hail as large softballs pummeled portions of Cowley county, with other numerous hail reports reaching golfball and baseball size across the area. Residence and car windows were cracked and shattered, commercial and residential properties sustained roof damage, and numerous vehicles sustained hail damage. The communities of Floral, Burden, Dexter and New Salem, all in Cowley County, were hardest hit. Unfortunately, an estimate on the total amount of damage inflicted is unknown. The storms weakened as they moved east to southeast into southeast Kansas, producing marginally severe hail. Additionally, areas of relatively minor flooding occurred due to numerous thunderstorms moving over the same areas.

Thunderstorms developed along a dryline during the late afternoon of May 1st, and sustained as the synoptic cold front dove through the region later that evening. Two tornadoes touched down and caused damage in portions of Osage and Douglas Counties.  One residence in Douglas County was almost completely demolished by one of the tornadoes, rated an EF-2. Hail from the size of quarters to the size of golfballs was also reported across portions of northeast and east central Kansas. After 11pm CST, the severe weather threat focus turned to strong winds. Trees were damaged across portions of the warning area, as was property including homes, outbuidings, power poles, and transformers. The Emergency Manager from Osage County reported that property damage estimates were around $100,000. Spotters estimated wind speeds up to 70 mph, and a measured gust of 71 mph was reported by the ASOS at the Lawrence Municipal Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a powerline strung to a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary boundary during the early morning hours of the 26th, and persisted for several hours. The city of Manahattan was hit particularly hard by the heavy rain. Streets across the city quickly filled, with storm drains too slow to drag the accumulating rain away fast enough. The Riley County Emergency manager reported that several streets in town were covered from curb to curb with water. The State Climatologist reported that nearly two inches of rain fell in a forty-five minute period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets in Manhattan were reported to have curb to curb flooding.  Vehicles were stranded at the intersection of Bertrand and Juliet. Portions of N. Manhattan Road and Lovers Lane were closed as a result of high water over roadways. Streets near 3rd St. were running full. The state climatologist reported 1.90 inches of rainfall in a forty-five minute period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough working across the region along with an upper level jet streak, helped to develop scattered thunderstorms across north central Kansas during the late afternoon hours on May 5th.  These thunderstorms produced some hail up to the size of quarters in Rooks and Osborne Counties.

A shortwave trough working across the region along with an upper level jet streak, helped to develop scattered thunderstorms across north central Kansas during the late afternoon hours on May 5th.  These thunderstorms produced some hail up to the size of quarters in Rooks and Osborne Counties.

Another severe weather episode moved across the forecast area producing widespread flooding and hail across most of the counties served.  A few high wind events were also noted.  Several high water rescues were noted during this time frame as people would drive into water covered roads and their vehicle would become inundated with water before finally retiring.  This caused for some consternation with local law and fire officials as they had to rescue the individuals from on top of their vehicle or in one instance they were clinging to a tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two water rescues were conducted in Coffeyville while many city roads were closed in Independence due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated convection developed on the nose of the low level jet late the evening of the 24th, and continued through the morning of the 25th. Severe hail was the primary threat with the elevated storms, but a lone, long track supercell the developed over the northwestern part of the state was able to sustain it's strength through Cloud County. A relatively long track tornado crossed through Jewell County to just south of Jamestown in Cloud County before lifting. EF-1 damage was sustained in the Topeka Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifty mile per hour winds also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough located over the western U.S. was ejecting pieces of energy out over the Central Plains. Ahead of this trough at the surface, low pressure developed over eastern Colorado, helping to push a warm front north into the forecast area. The combination of these weather systems brought thunderstorms to north-central Kansas. Storms first initiated over western Kansas along the dry line, with more development later on along the warm front. Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours and continued on well into the evening. This event was also the first in a series of nights in which many areas saw heavy rain.

Severe thunderstorms developed over NW Kansas during the early evening hours. One long-lived supercell moved slowly east across Sheridan and Graham counties, producing baseball-sized hail and winds of 70-80 mph. Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Follow-up newspaper reports indicated significant window and roof damage in the town of Selden. Emergency manager estimated around 75% of the homes in Selden had some type of window or siding damage on the north side.  Most hail was of golf ball size with the largest being softball. The grade school was closed the following day due to roof and water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in eastern Colorado created a tight pressure gradient across the area.  The result was strong south winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report of strong wind was relayed by KSNW and measured at the weather lab in Stafford.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Western Kansas and eventually morphed into a bow echo that moved through parts of Central Kansas. This cluster of storms eventually was overtaken by another severe line of storms that moved through South Central Kansas. Although there were a few hail reports the biggest hazard from these storms were severe straight-line winds. The remnants from these storms moved into Southeast Kansas causing minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported on Main Street in Erie as well as other low lying roads in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into north central Kansas early the morning of the 24th. Hail to the size of half dollars fell, and winds to 70 mph were reported across the region. Minor damage was reported, mainly to tree limbs, power lines, and some structures. One home had it's roof partially torn off, and it's carport destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was blown over near the begnning of the damage path, and a barn collapsed onto a tractor near the end.  Trees were reported down along the path, and a home on Nickel Road has it's roof partially torn off. The same home had it's carport destroyed. Tree limbs were reported down across Belleville. A house near the intersection of N and 24th Streets had a large limb fall onto a nearby power line and porch. A wall on the south side of the Belleville Car Wash was also blown down by the strong winds.  Wind damage continued along a swath from 6 miles south of Belleville to Hanover in Washington County.  Near Hanover, minor damage was reported across the town. A tree fell onto the roof of a home on the west side of the home, causing damage to a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough located over the western U.S. was ejecting pieces of energy out over the Central Plains. Ahead of this trough at the surface, low pressure developed over eastern Colorado, helping to push a warm front north into the forecast area. The combination of these weather systems brought thunderstorms to north-central Kansas. Storms first initiated over western Kansas along the dry line, with more development later on along the warm front. Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours and continued on well into the evening. This event was also the first in a series of nights in which many areas saw heavy rain.

A powerful late Spring upper-level system moved into the Central Plains during the early afternoon of June 5th. It appeared that a major tornado outbreak would occur from Nebraska into Oklahoma. Instead storms quickly evolved into bowing line segments producing mainly high winds. Isolated very intense winds occurred along mesovortices across portions of South Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten power poles were blown down and 4 roofs were blow off of out buildings. Winds were estimated at 100 mph. Golf ball sized hail also was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked down 4 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning and afternoon hours of June 15th, a large Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed across portions of Nebraska, Iowa, and Kansas, and dropped southeast through the area. There were several reports of severe weather. Winds between 60 to 75 mph were reported in portions of Schuyler, Sullivan, Grundy, and Nodaway counties, during the late morning hours.

Supercell thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary across Central and South Central Kansas during the late afternoon hours.  The highly sheared environment lead to the supercells producing tornadoes between Wichita, Kansas and Winfield Kansas. The supercell thunderstorms evolved into a complex of thunderstorms during the early morning hours of June 13th, 2008. The complex of thunderstorms moved slowly over Southeast Kansas producing flash flooding.

A surface cold front moving in from the northwest stalled out over the Central Plains, providing a focus for thunderstorm development across north central Kansas.  Conditions were favorable for thunderstorms to become strong to severe, but capping aloft delayed development until the late afternoon hours.  Severe thunderstorms across the area dropped hail to the size of ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief touchdown was reported.

A potent upper level disturbance and associated frontal boundary sparked strong to severe thunderstorms from the mid morning through the afternoon hours of April 23rd across central, south-central and southeast Kansas. Hail as large as golfballs and winds up to 60 mph were reported. Additionally, slow moving and training thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall amounts, particularly across the northern half of Marion County, where excessive runoff caused the North Cottonwood River to overflow its banks, flooding the town of Durham with around 18 inches of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to golfball size hail occurred.

Elevated convection developed on the nose of the low level jet late the evening of the 24th, and continued through the morning of the 25th. Severe hail was the primary threat with the elevated storms, but a lone, long track supercell the developed over the northwestern part of the state was able to sustain it's strength through Cloud County. A relatively long track tornado crossed through Jewell County to just south of Jamestown in Cloud County before lifting. EF-1 damage was sustained in the Topeka Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado crossed into Cloud County and caused damage across mainly rural areas. Several power poles were snapped off, an abandonded home was damaged, and several out-buildings were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated strong cold front sparked a few severe thunderstorms across south-central Kansas during the mid to late afternoon hours of April 26th. Hail as large as quarters and winds up to 64 mph were reported across portions of Reno and Sedgwick counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stillwell Road closed just west of the intersection of Stillwell Road and 166th due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system, brought severe thunderstorms with an isolated tornado, high winds, and hail, to the area on June 5, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large oak trees were blown down and a high school goal post was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system, brought severe thunderstorms with an isolated tornado, high winds, and hail, to the area on June 5, 2008.
A complex storm system, brought severe thunderstorms with an isolated tornado, high winds, and hail, to the area on June 5, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power was lost in the city of Elwood with several trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms erupted along a strong cold front across Central Kansas.  The supercell storms produced destructive hail and damaging winds, along with tornadoes across Central, Kansas.  A few strong tornadoes touched down just south of Salina, Kansas.  The supercells would continue to track to the northeast and eventually produce the tornadoes that would hit Chapman, Kansas and Manhattan, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread damage was noted to several homes, businesses, and trees along the tornadoes path. One cabinet making business had a 10000 square foot section of roof removed, with a total of 20000 square feet of damage to the structure as a sprinkler main broke pouring water onto the factory floor.  Several homes sustained damage due to garage doors collapsing in the wind and ultimately lifting the roofs off of the homes.  One home sustained slightly more damage than the rest, as two exterior walls were also blown out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms developed over Northwest Kansas on the evening and overnight of June 18th, 2008.  The complex of storms lead to a quasi linear convective system moving south across portions of Central and South Central Kansas,  producing damaging winds along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KSNW television weather lab recorded a wind gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into north central Kansas early the morning of the 24th. Hail to the size of half dollars fell, and winds to 70 mph were reported across the region. Minor damage was reported, mainly to tree limbs, power lines, and some structures. One home had it's roof partially torn off, and it's carport destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two grain bins were damaged near the intersection of Highway 50 and Emmer Road. One of these bins was blown across a roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought a cold front through eastern half of Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 1st. The front was the trigger for initial severe thunderstorms in Southeast Kansas. These storms produced several short-lived tornadoes as well as large hail and damaging winds. Another line of severe storms quickly developed along the mid-level front over South Central Kansas. This line of storms moved to east across all of Southeast Kansas but its intensity weakened as it crossed the Flint Hills. The second round of storms produced large, high winds and very heavy rain.

A subtle upper level disturbance interacted with a frontal boundary and unstable airmass to produce numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across  much of central and south-central Kansas, as well as portions of southeast Kansas from the afternoon hours of the 25th through the early morning hours of the 26th. Four tornadoes touched down across central Kansas, but fortunately they occurred over open country and produced no known damage. Additionally, hail as large as golfballs occurred across central Kansas, and an intense microburst with winds up to 100 mph caused a narrow and short swath of damage across West Wichita.

Supercell thunderstorms developed across South Central Kansas late on Saturday, May 10th, 2008, and moved into portions Southeast Kansas. The storms produced very large hail up to tennis ball size.  Two supercells moved across Labette County, Kansas, one producing a brief tornado touchdown before moving into Cherokee County, Kansas, and the other a tornado 2 miles south of the Kansas/Oklahoma border south of Chetopa, Kansas.  This supercell would eventually produce the EF4 tornado that struck Picher, Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported near Elk City Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two supercells initiated over Kansas before turning deadly over Oklahoma and Missouri.  One tornado associated with the northern supercell touched down near Faulkner.  This storm later caused a fatality near Carthage, MO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey confirmed that an EF0 tornado tracked through the Faulkner area. Damage was limited to minor damage to the grain elevator and a nearby home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the area during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Long-track tornadoes, flash flooding, large hail and damaging winds were reported across much of northwest Kansas into extreme southern Nebraska.  Eleven tornadoes were verified by storm chasers and damage surveys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed 3 miles SW of Grainfield and moved north 11 miles into Sheridan county, dissipating 9 miles SW of Hoxie. This tornado remained over open fields.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved into southern Sheridan county from Gove county, and remained over open fields until dissipation 9 miles SW of Hoxie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the region during the afternoon and evening hours producing 10 tornadoes, including an F4 tornado just SW of Quinter. Extremely heavy rainfall also resulted in flooding in many areas. Many of these thunderstorms occurred in the same areas as the storms the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved into southern Sheridan county from Gove county.  It initially moved NNE, then straight north on Road 120E then turned back to the NNW before dissipating near Road 50S and 100E in Sheridan county.  Approximately five miles of utility poles were broken or heavily damaged from a combination of tornado and rear flank downdraft winds.  In addition, several farmsteads suffered damage to the home and outbuildings.  A mobile home was blown off its supports and into a nearby home which was destroyed as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same supercell thunderstorm which produced the tornado across SW-C Decatur county developed a second tornado about 3 miles SW of Oberlin. This tornado moved north and and dissipated one mile SE of Cedar Falls, traveling along the west side of highway 83.  This tornado remained mostly over open fields, but destroyed a number of irrigation pivots and damaged.  The rear flank downdraft caused damage in several locations on highway 83, including grain bins at a feed lot and outbuildings at a farmstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the area during the afternoon and early evening hours. Eleven tornadoes were verified by storm chasers and damage surveys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed just south of highway 24 west of Hoxie, and moved north nine miles before dissipating. It produced EF2 damage at a home on highway 24, removing much of the roof of the home and destroying a large metal outbuilding. Significant tree damage also occurred.  South of the highway, an irrigation pivot was overturned and a second home suffered minor damage and a small outbuilding was destroyed.

Supercell thunderstorms developed across South Central Kansas late on Saturday, May 10th, 2008, and moved into portions Southeast Kansas. The storms produced very large hail up to tennis ball size.  Two supercells moved across Labette County, Kansas, one producing a brief tornado touchdown before moving into Cherokee County, Kansas, and the other a tornado 2 miles south of the Kansas/Oklahoma border south of Chetopa, Kansas.  This supercell would eventually produce the EF4 tornado that struck Picher, Oklahoma.

A subtle upper level disturbance interacted with a frontal boundary and unstable airmass to produce numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across  much of central and south-central Kansas, as well as portions of southeast Kansas from the afternoon hours of the 25th through the early morning hours of the 26th. Four tornadoes touched down across central Kansas, but fortunately they occurred over open country and produced no known damage. Additionally, hail as large as golfballs occurred across central Kansas, and an intense microburst with winds up to 100 mph caused a narrow and short swath of damage across West Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado touchdown in open country.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense microburst with winds estimated at around 100 mph caused a narrow and short swath of damage just east of the 13th and West intersection. An apartment complex carport was blown over and caused damage to around 25 cars, and some apartment complex windows were blown out. Additionally, approximately 4 to 6 trees measuring about 5 to 8 inches in diameter were downed or even snapped mid trunk. Path length of the intense microburst was about 100 yards.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed across north-central Oklahoma and south-central Kansas from the evening hours of May 24th through the early morning hours of the 25th, ahead of a strong dryline draped across the central and southern plains. Activity then spread east to northeast across south-central Kansas and into the Flint Hills. Hail as large as golfballs occurred as well as winds up to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported by KSNW television.

Two supercells moved across Labette County, Kansas, one producing a brief tornado touchdown before moving into Cherokee County, Kansas, and the other a tornado 2 miles south of the Kansas/Oklahoma border south of Chetopa, Kansas.  This supercell would eventually produce the EF4 tornado that struck Picher, Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported on the north side of the city.

A strong low pressure system brought a cold front through eastern half of Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 1st. The front was the trigger for initial severe thunderstorms in Southeast Kansas. These storms produced several short-lived tornadoes as well as large hail and damaging winds. Another line of severe storms quickly developed along the mid-level front over South Central Kansas. This line of storms moved to east across all of Southeast Kansas but its intensity weakened as it crossed the Flint Hills. The second round of storms produced large, high winds and very heavy rain.

A surface low pressure area moved northeast out of Colorado during the day.  Severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours and continued well into the evening. Nearly 50 reports of severe weather were received in NW Kansas with a few hail reports in SW Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down in Oakley, including a large tree at city hall.  Power lines reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly dime sized but a few stones around the size of a quarter fell as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle upper level disturbance interacted with a frontal boundary and unstable airmass to produce numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across  much of central and south-central Kansas, as well as portions of southeast Kansas from the afternoon hours of the 25th through the early morning hours of the 26th. Four tornadoes touched down across central Kansas, but fortunately they occurred over open country and produced no known damage. Additionally, hail as large as golfballs occurred across central Kansas, and an intense microburst with winds up to 100 mph caused a narrow and short swath of damage across West Wichita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into north central Kansas early the morning of the 24th. Hail to the size of half dollars fell, and winds to 70 mph were reported across the region. Minor damage was reported, mainly to tree limbs, power lines, and some structures. One home had it's roof partially torn off, and it's carport destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bahr Storage storage unit was destroyed, and debris from the building was scattered across Hwy 75. Of note, the town's mural was painted on the east side of this now destroyed building. Several power lines were also snapped.

A weakening thunderstorm in Lyon County produced a heat burst and attendant strong winds that caused damage south of the city of Emporia.  The measured temperature at the Emporia Airport (KEMP) increased by 20 degrees between 4:44am and 5:11am from 71 to 91 degrees fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down along 160 Road just south of the city of Emporia. Power was reported to be out across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, weak thunderstorms developed in the warm advection regime ahead of a surface cold front. The storms were not severe, but a teenage girl was injured when a lightning bolt struck near her home, entered the piping, and wounded her.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15-year-old girl was struck by lightning when a bolt hit near her home, traveled through the steel plumbing, and into the shower nozzle.  The girl was taking a shower at the time she was struck. A single indentation was evident on the girl's knee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms again developed across portions of central and north central Kansas the morning of the 23rd, and lingered through around 10am CST. A single report of hail the size of pennies was received by the office with the most impressive of the otherwise weak storms.

This line of storms moved to east across all of Southeast Kansas but its intensity weakened as it crossed the Flint Hills. The second round of storms produced large, high winds and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred southeast of the intersection of 21st Street North and Maize.

One storm kept redeveloping over Cherokee County.  Large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across the county.  More specifically, Lightning Creek, Cherry Creek, and Fly Creek all flooded low lying areas and several roads.  Street flooding occurred in Baxter Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms impacted extreme southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts ripped a roof off an outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that died early in the day on May 5th left a an outflow boundary across Central Kansas. As a weak upper wave moved over the boundary additional thunderstorms developed during the evening hours and moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly pea to dime sized hail fell but a few stones of up to quarter size were reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense supercell thunderstorms moved north across the region during the afternoon and evening hours producing 10 tornadoes, including an F4 tornado just SW of Quinter. Extremely heavy rainfall also resulted in flooding in many areas. Many of these thunderstorms occurred in the same areas as the storms the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado was described as a thick rope with an estimated width of 100 yards.  tornado developed near Road 50 and Road A, dissipating near Road 50 and Road F.  The tornado remained over open country with no known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the low level jet overnight the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. The extremely moist airmass in place lead to efficiant rainfall producing thunderstorms. Local Doppler Radar rainfall estimates showed that four to six inches of rain had fallen across portions of Jackson, Jefferson, and Nemaha counties during the overnight hours. One report from a spotter in Whiting noted that eight inches of precipitation had fallen through the night. The heavy rain and associated run-off caused problems across these counties, from water flowing over roadways to filled drainage ditches and crop fields. At least one bridge was damaged by the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over several roadways in the Valley Falls area. The cooperative observer located three miles southwest of Valley Falls reported 3.76 inches of rain as of 6:10am CST. The intersection of 142nd and K-4 had an estimated 6 inches of water over the roadway.  Blue Mound Road north of K-16 was closed because of high water over the roadway. The Delaware River on the north side of Valley Falls was running two feet out of it's banks.  A roadway near the intersection of Coal Creek Road and 170th was covered with one and a half feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved into north central Kansas early the morning of the 24th. Hail to the size of half dollars fell, and winds to 70 mph were reported across the region. Minor damage was reported, mainly to tree limbs, power lines, and some structures. One home had it's roof partially torn off, and it's carport destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inch diameter live tree limbs breaking as a result of the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper system in the westerlies combined with a strong subtropical jet over southwest Kansas.  At the surface there was a dryline and front intersection allowing deep convective initiation.

A very strong low pressure center and high pressure moving down into the central Plains produced a tight pressure gradient over western Kansas.  In addition, mixing of the boundary layer allowed strong winds aloft to reach the surface.  Visibilities in blowing dust lowered to under 1 mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi truck was blown over on highway 281 south of St. John.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough off of Baja ejected into the plains and combined with deep surface moisture and a dryline to produce widespread severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 4.00 inches was recorded at a location 5 miles east of Greensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Jet streak dynamics associated with an anonymously strong upper level low in the rockies interacted with increasing low level moisture to produced widespread severe thunderstorms.  The first tornadoes of the season occurred on this afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado curved back to the northwest before dissipating.  It caused EF1 damage at a farm where a 40 by 60 foot shed was destroyed.  The tornado also took down fencing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final severe weather outbreak produced from the slow moving upper level disturbance produced hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 damage was done to trees and a pivot irrigation sprinkler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final severe weather outbreak produced from the slow moving upper level disturbance produced hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a brief tornado that last about 30 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final severe weather outbreak produced from the slow moving upper level disturbance produced hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado did not apparently come in contact with anything or was too weak to cause damage.  It was video taped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final severe weather outbreak produced from the slow moving upper level disturbance produced hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This video taped tornado produced EF1 damage to trees and power poles.

A few strong to severe thunderstorms impacted portions of extreme southeast Kansas and west central Missouri.  These storms caused wind damaged to trees, a grain bin, and a farm truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph destroyed a grain bin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe thunderstorms impacted portions of extreme southeast Kansas and west central Missouri.  These storms caused wind damaged to trees, a grain bin, and a farm truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rain caused flooding of low lying roads near the communities of Fulton and Devon.  A section of Highway 31 approximately five miles west of Fulton was closed to motorists due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe thunderstorms impacted portions of extreme southeast Kansas and west central Missouri.  These storms caused wind damaged to trees, a grain bin, and a farm truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rain caused nearly all low water crossings across the county to flood.  The Pittsburg area appeared to have been impacted with the greatest flooding as several city streets were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe thunderstorms impacted portions of extreme southeast Kansas and west central Missouri.  These storms caused wind damaged to trees, a grain bin, and a farm truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All low lying roads and areas that typically flood during episodes of flooding were flooded.  The Emergency Management Director stated that there were too many flooded locations to specify to the NWS.  Streams that flooded multiple locations included Cow, Lightning, Brush, Fly, and Cherry creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms erupted along a strong cold front across Central Kansas.  The supercell storms produced destructive hail and damaging winds, along with tornadoes across Central, Kansas.  A few strong tornadoes touched down just south of Salina, Kansas.  The supercells would continue to track to the northeast and eventually produce the tornadoes that would hit Chapman, Kansas and Manhattan, Kansas.

This episode of severe weather and flooding occurred during the overnight period of 22 June, and into the morning hours of 23 June.  A nocturnal low level jet developed over the southern and central Plains and interacted with a stalled front extending from east central Missouri to southeast Kansas.  Hail to the size of baseballs occurred from Cherokee County, Kansas to Jasper County, Missouri.  Several observations of wind gusts to 70 mph also occurred over southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations across Cherokee County experienced flash flooding due to excessive rain.  A few specific locations that flooded included a section of Centerstar Road, a section of Highway 7 north of Columbus, a section of Southwest 110th Street and a section of Southwest 120th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms ravaged portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas during the overnight hours of June 26th. Thunderstorms initially developed over Nebraska and northern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th. This activity then surged southeast into an increasingly moist and unstable airmass, producing widespread damaging winds, along with areas of very heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some utility poles were downed in and around Udall. Information courtesy of the Winfield Courier.

Aided by a stout low level jet, this MCS dropped hail from the size of pennies to golf balls across north central Kansas, with a report of baseball size hail near Webber in Jewell County.  Locally heavy also accompanied these storms.  As the low level jet weakened during the mid morning hours, thunderstorm intensity was reduced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accompanied by wind gusts estimated at 40 to 50 MPH.

The supercells would continue to track to the northeast and eventually produce the tornadoes that would hit Chapman, Kansas and Manhattan, Kansas.
